Subject:

Chad—Fifth Review Under the Three-Year Arrangement Under the Poverty Reduction and Growth Facility, and Requests for Waiver of Performance Criteria, Extension of the Commitment Period Under the PRGF, and Extension of Interim Assistance Under the Enhanced HIPC Initiative

Attached for consideration by the Executive Directors is a paper on the fifth review under the three-year arrangement under the Poverty Reduction and Growth Facility for Chad, and requests for waiver of performance criteria, extension of the commitment period under the PRGF, and extension of interim assistance under the enhanced HIPC Initiative, which is tentatively scheduled for discussion on Monday, June 23, 20o3. Draft decisions appear on pages 32-36. At the time of circulation of this paper to the Board, the Secretary's Department has not received a communication from the authorities of Chad indicating whether or not they consent to the Fund's publication of this paper; such communication may be received after the authorities have had an opportunity to read the paper.
Questions may be referred to Mr. Georgiou (ext. 36099), Mr. Karangwa (ext. 38612), and Mr. Dauphin (ext. 39705) in AFR.

Unless the Documents Section (ext. 36760) is otherwise notified, the document will be transmitted, in accordance with the procedures approved by the Executive Board and with the appropriate deletions, to the WTO Secretariat on Wednesday, June 18, 2003; and to the African Development Bank, the European Commission, the Islamic Development Bank, and the Organisation for Economic Cooperation and Development, following its consideration by the Executive Board.
This document will be posted on the extranet shortly.

INTERNATIONALMONETARYFUND
CHAD

Fifth Review Under the Three-Year Arrangement Under the Poverty Reduction and Growth Facility, and Requests for Waiver of Performance Criteria, Extension of the Commitment Period Under the PRGF, and Extension of Interim Assistance Under the Enhanced HIPC Initiative
Prepared by the African Department (In consultation with the Fiscal Affairs, Legal, Monetary and Financial Systems, Policy Development and Review, Statistics, and Finance Departments)
Approved by Amor Tahari and Michael Hadjimichael

The three-year PRGF arrangement was approved on January 7, 2000, for an amount equivalent to SDR 36.4 million. This amount was subsequently augmented to 47.6 million, and the commitment period was extended to December 6, 2003. The fourth review was concluded on October 18, 2002. In total, an amount equivalent to SDR 37.2 millon has been disbursed under the arrangement. This staff report proposes the conclusion of the fifth review, the disbursement of an amount equivalent to SDR 5.2 million, and a further extension of the commitment period to January 6, 2004.

Chad reached thc HIPC Initiative decision point on May 16, 200l. The Fund's committed assistance is equivalent to SDR 14.25 million, of which the equivalent of SDR 4.33 million has already been disbursed. The interim assistance was extended for one year on May l6, 2002. This staffreport proposes to grant a further one-year extension.

A mission to conduct the discussons of the fifth PRGF arrangement review visited N'Djamena during April l-14, 2003. The mission met with Prime Minister Haroun Kabadi, Minister of Economy and Finance Idriss Ahmed Idriss, Minister of Plan Djimrangar Dadnadji, National Director of the regional central bank (BEAC) Mahamat Mustapha; and other senior officials.

The team comprised Messrs. Georgiou (head), Karangwa, and Dauphin, and Mss. Strauss, Amega, and Allain (Resident Representative) (all AFR).The mission worked closely with overlapping missions from FAD and the World Bank.

Political situation. The current government has been in place since June 2OO2; there was a minor reshuffling of it on May 6, 2003. Despite the signature of a peace agreement with the main rebellion movement in January 2002 and the subsequent surrender of a number of armed groups, rebel activity has persisted in various parts of the country. The government continues to hold discussions with armed groups that stillhold out. Relations between Chad and the Central African Republic (CAR) deteriorated in the course of 2002 but have improved since the change of government in the CAR in March 2003.

EXECUTIVE SUMMARY

Performance vis- $\mathbf{\dot{a}}$ -vis the program was broadly satisfactory. Most quantitative indicative targets and performance criteria at end September and end-December 2002 were met. Two structural performance criteria were observed, but one structural performance criterion and two structural benchmarks were not. There was an incident of misreporting, for which a separate report has been prepared.

Economic performance in 2002 was good overall, but some risks have emerged. Despite a drought that affected agricultural production, real GDP growth was close to 10 percent in 2002, spearheaded by investment in the oil sector. Inflation was above target, fueled by higher-thanexpected money growth on account of unsterilized capital inflows. This, combined with the appreciation of the euro against the dollar, led to a strong real effective appreciation of the currency in 2002. Inflation pressures and the loss of competitiveness need to be monitored closely and identified measures implemented, if developments warrant it.

The broad fiscal targets were achieved in 20o2, but there were problems with revenue collection and the composition of spending. While the overall revenue target was met, underlying weaknesses emerged in many tax areas, as reforms lagged and exemptions were abused. The execution of the 2002 budget did not reflect the government's stated priorities, as poverty-reducing spending fellsignificantly below target, while spending in other sectors was at or above program targets. Beyond identified remedial measures, a strong political commitment willbe the key to successfully addressing these two major concerns.

Decompression of nonpriority expenditure and a catch-up in priority sector spending drive the revision of the 2003 fiscal program. As earlier envisaged, additional foreign financing will allow incrcascs in budgeted spending for 2003 in nonpriority (and, to some extent, in priority) areas. The priority sector spending catch-up for 2002 will be financed by the savings from the weak execution of the fully financed 2002 budget.

Progress has been slow in structural reforms. While good progress was made in the area of transparency, progress has been uneven in other areas of governance, in fiscal expenditure management, and in the reforms of the cotton sector and the civil service.

Major progress was made in the preparation for the oil era with the design of a comprehensive set of modalities for the use of oil revenue for poverty reduction. These modalities aim at sheltering spending from the volatility and exhaustibility of oil revenue; ensuring the effective absorption of spending in the priority sectors; sterilizing savings accumulated for stabilization purposes; ensuring an adequate monitoring of the use of oil revenue; and guaranteeing the true intergenerational character of the Fund for Future Generations.

The full PRSP was completed and submitted to the Fund and the World Bank on June 6, 2003. Strong efforts are needed to correct for the insufficient progress observed in most of the social indicators that constitute triggers for Chad's reaching HIPC Initiative completion point in June 2004, as envisaged by the authorities.
I. INTRODUCTION

1. On January 7, 2000, the Executive Board approved Chad's request for a three-year arrangement under the Poverty Reduction and Growth Facility (PRGF) in an amount equivalent to SDR 36.4 million (65 percent of quota). On October 18, 2002, the Board concluded the fourth review, extended the commitment period under the arrangement by eleven months to December 6, 2003, and approved the seventh disbursement under the arrangement. A mission to conduct the discussions of the fifth review under the PRGF arrangement visited N'Djaména during April 1--14, 2003.2

2. In the attached letter of intent (LOI) to the Managing Director (Appendix I), dated June 10, 2o03, the Chadian authorities request (i) a waiver for the nonobservance of a quantitative and a structural performance criterion and the completion of the fifth review; (ii) the extension of the commitment period under the current arrangement; and (ii) the extension of the interim assistance under the enhanced Initiative for Heavily Indebted Poor Countries (HIPC Initiative). A memorandum of economic and financial policies (MEFP) is attached to the LOI (Appendix I, Attachment I). Prior actions for completing the fifth review are set out in paragraph 40 of Appendix I, Attachment I, and a set of quantitative and structural benchmarks and performance criteria for the remainder of 2o03 is presented in Tables l and 3 of Appendix I, Attachment I, respectively. An amendment and an addendum to the technical memorandum of understanding (TMU) are also attached to Appendix I (Attachments II and II). Chad's relations with the Fund and the World Bank are summarized in Appendices II and III, respectively; Appendix IV provides a summary of progress in meeting the enhanced HIPC Initiative completion point conditions.

3. The full poverty reduction strategy paper (PRSP) was finalized and formally submitted to the Fund and World Bank on June 6, 2003. The need to procccd in a timely fashion with the completion of the fifth review did not permit preparation of the joint staff assessment (JSA) in time for the Board meeting on this staff report. The PRSP has now been published and circulated to the Board. The staf, therefore, proposes that some flexibility be exercised with regard to the requirement of a status report on PRSP preparation and, keeping with current practice, the JSA/PRSP be considered by the Board by October 2003.
II. RECENT DEVELOPMENTS

4. The government has continued to face a number of security challenges. Rebel activity has persisted in various parts of the country, even while various armed groups have taken advantage of the opportunities offered by the demobilization/reintegration plan, besides those of the MDJT (Mouvement pour la Democratie et la Justice au Tchad) who signed the January 2002 peace accord. The government is continuing discussions with groups that are still holding out. Relations between Chad and the Central African Republic (CAR) deteriorated in 2002, but appear to have improved since the change of government in the CAR in March 2003.

5. Quantitative indicative targets and performance criteria were for the most part observed, but performance vis-a-vis structural conditions was more mixed (see paras. 2-3 of the LOl). All quantitative indicative targets and performance criteria for endSeptember and end-December 2002 were observed, except for the continuous performance criterion on the nonaccumulation of new external payments arrears and the indicative targets relating to spending in health and education (Appendix I, Attachment I, Table 1). Two structural performance criteria were observed, but one structural performance criterion and two structural benchmarks were not (Appendix I, Attachment I, Table 2). The missed structural performance criterion concerns the adoption of a customs action plan; the missed structural benchmarks concern the inclusion in the 2003 budget of the medium-term expenditure program for the health and education sectors, and the modalities for the use of oi resources.

6. Chad received a noncomplying disbursement following the completion of the fourth review in October 2002, owing to the provision of inaccurate information on new external payments arrears. A report by the Managing Director to the Executive Board (EBS/03/38; 3/28/03) and a supplement to the report detail these problems and include a recommendation of the Managing Director to the Board. In the context of their MEFP (para. 23), the authorities have committed themselves to implementing measures to prevent the recurrence of new external payments arrears, as well as of misreporting. Clearing all external payments arrears and implementing all identified remedial measures are prior actions for the completion of this fifth review.

7. Economic performance in 20o2 was good overall, but some risks have emerged. Spearheaded by investment in the oil sector, real GDP growth reached 9.7 percent in 2002; this level was, however, below program projections because of a drought (Table 1). The average annual rate of inflation, based on data collected in N'Djaména, reached 5.2 percent, compared with a programmed level of 4 percent. Inflation in the country as a whole is likely to have been even higher owing to stronger price increases, at least in the region of the oil pipeline project.4 Inflation and the nominal appreciation of the euro against the dollar led to a strong real effective appreciation of the currency in 2002, roughly estimated in the range of 10-12 percent (Figure 1). The strong demand was supported by a 24.2 percent increase in broad money in 2002, compared with 15 percent in the monetary program (Table 6). A larger-than-programmed increase in net foreign assets was responsible for this development, and reflected the fact that a large part of the foreign exchange inflows associated with the pipeline project were not spent on imports, as expected.

8. The broad fiscal targets were achieved in 2oo2, but the compositions of spending and revenue were less satisfactory. The base primary and overall fiscal deficits were below the program targets, reflecting higher-than-programmed revenue and an overall low rate of implementation of expenditure (Tables 2 and 3). The good overall revenue performance6 was on account of buoyant returns of income taxes from companies connected with the accelerating Doba oil project, which more than offset weak tax collection in virtually all other areas (0.7 percent of GDP below target).7 The weaknesses are explained not only by delays in implementing envisaged revenue-increasing measures, but also by an abuse of tax exemptions granted in the context of the pipeline project. The low rate of implementation of expenditure affected mostly the priority sectors, with undesirable effects for the composition of spending. Expenditures in nonpriority areas proceeded as planned. Moreover, nonwage current military spending was significantly higher than originally budgeted, following the
decision to purchase 0.3 percent of GDP worth of equipment to replace that destroyed in October during a rebel attack on Faya (Table 2).8

y. The authorities reported that expenditure related to the demobilization ot rebels operating in various parts of the country amounted to CFAF 2.5 billion in 2002. Out of this amount, CFAF 2.1 billion covered the implementation of the peace agreement signed with the MDJT and could therefore be included in the adjuster to the performance criteria on net credit to government and the base primary fiscal balance. The authorities report that 538 MDJT rebels had been demobilized and another 574 reintegrated into the army. Under the government's policy of national reconciliation, the remaining CFAF 0.4 billion was spent for the demobilization of 72 rebels and the reintegration of another 100 from a rebel movement (ANDR: Aliance Nationale de la Resistance) operating in the east of the country. At the same time, the authorities acknowledged that some individuals may have pretended to be former rebels so as to enjoy the benefits associated with the demobilization/reintegration plan; however, they did not screen these individuals vigorously, as they wanted to encourage the surrender of as many armed groups as possible.

10. There was a significant shortfall in external financing in 20o2, mostly due to the delays in fulfilling World Bank conditions. At the time of this shortfall, there was a buildup of new external payments arrears, a smaller-than-programmed reduction of domestic arrears, and shortfalls in priority current expenditure and domestically financed investment. As a result, net credit to government was significantly below the adjusted program target, in spite of the CFAF 3 billion exceptional advance from the Bank of Central African States (BEAC) used to finance the aforementioned unanticipated replacement of military equipment (see footnote 8).

11. The adverse composition of expenditure points to weak administrative capacity but also raises some concerns about priorities. The authorities argued that the unfavorable composition of spending could be attributed to two causes: (i) administrative capacity was weaker in the social sectors than in the nonpriority sectors; and (ii) budget execution in these

sectors more often required complex bidding procedures because of the much larger size of their budgets. They also explained that the execution of priority expenditures in the 2002 budget was even worse on a cash basis than on a commitment basis because some payments could not be complctcd bcfore the end of 2002. The staff feels that the low rate of execution of priority spending reflects not only a generalized shortage of

qualified personnel, but also a lack of focus on an adequate distribution of resources for administration among priority and nonpriority sectors. Inappropriate prioritization of spending in response to the large shortfall in foreign financing may have also played a role.

12. Little progress has been made in addressing weaknesses in public expenditure management, including in the preparation of the monthly cash-flow plan, the use of the contingency mechanism set up to shield priority sector expenditure from financing shortfalls, the computerization of the treasury, and the preparation of the table monitoring the four stages of the expenditure process (para. 8 of the MEFP and Box 1 of this report). Undoubtcdly, thcse deficiencies were part of the causes underlying the poor composition of expenditure and arrears problems.

13. Progress in structural reforms in the area of fiscal revenue collection was mixed. While some steps were taken (para. 7 of the MEFP) in preparing the reforms of the customs administration and livestock taxation, as well as toward producing a tax procedure handbook, past measures to strengthen the ex post verification of tax exemptions did not generate the hoped-for results. In addition, the low priority surprisingly assigncd by thc government to cotton sector issues meant that the mechanism for value-added tax (VAT) credit reimbursement (virtually allto the state cotton company, Cotontchad) was not made operational, in spite of repeated commitments under the program, and VAT credit due for reimbursement accumulated again in 2002.

14. Progress in governance was most visible in the area of transparency, but in other areas it was fragmentary and slow. A number of important audits were published, as envisaged under the program (para. 9 of the MEFP). Moreover, an audit of the expenditures financed with savings from the enhanced HIPC Initiative in the second half of 2002 was launched. However, the recommendations of the previous audits were not fully implemented. This is evidenced by reports of the continuing use of limited bidding, fractioning of contracts, and direct awarding of contracts, as well as the weak drive to prosecute officials allegedly

Moreover, the preparation of the new procurement legislation was slower than hoped for, and the 200l budget setlement law (inclusive of the assessment of the Supreme Court) was not presented to parliament before the adoption of the 2003 budget. This presentation was delayed because the law was not accompanied by the assessment of the Supreme Court, as the report by the Ministry of Finance on budget execution was produced and submitted to the latter institution with significant delays (para. 9 of the MEFP).

15. The search for additional oil resources raised a number of issues. The government extended to the consortium that is currently exploiting the Doba oil fields the exclusive rights to prospect for oil in southern Chad, and it has been pursuing discussions with the same consortium, with a view to rapidly signing a second exploitation agreement. In communications with President Deby, the managements of the Fund and the World Bank inquired about issues of transparency and competitiveness in this area, and emphasized the importance of maximizing the benefit for Chad of any new exploitation agreement. The authorities indicated their intention to seek appropriate technical assistance in the context of their negotiations. The managements of the Fund and the World Bank also encouraged the authorities to use revenues from any discoveries of new oil fields in a way similar to that guiding the use of revenue from the Doba oil fields.

16. Cotton sector reform has continued to progress slowly. The draft study on various scenarios for the full divestiture of Cotontchad was completed in early December 2002, but the privatization of the former soap and oil division of Cotontchad was not completed as envisaged in March 2003. The delays in implementing the cotton sector reform, which was initiated in 1999, along with the low level of world market prices for cotton, have contributed to the continuing deterioration in the financial situation of Cotontchad and the declining incomes of cotton farmers. This situation has given rise to important government subsidies to Cotontchad in recent years. This said, shortfalls in the government's meeting of its commitments to the company (see para. 13) have left Cotonchad in a vulnerable financial condition. However, progress was made in the poverty and social impact analysis (PSIA) of the cotton sector reform, the results of which willinform the decisions on the privatization of Cotontchad (Box 2).

17. With respect to civil service reform, progress in some areas has been mixed with delays and backtrackings in other areas. While the audit of nine pilot ministries was recently launched, the implementation decrees of the new statutes of the civil service are not yet effective, and the payroll has not yet been computerized. Moreover, some measures recently taken by the government are contrary to the spirit of the civil service reform. The across-the-board salary increases for 2003 and 2004 recently awarded by the government appear to run counter to the principle of merit-based salary increases envisaged in the reform, apart from having potentially adverse budgetary implications over the medium term (see para. 23 below). In addition, the appointment, in the context of the recent breaking up of local administrative units, of offcials as“contractuals" who are outside the civil service, runs
Box 2. Chad: Status of the Poverty and Social Impact Analysis (PSIA) of the Cotton Sector Reform

A PSIA of the cotton sector reform in Chad is under way with technical and financial assistance from the World Bank. The results of that analysis are intended to inform policy choices regarding the design of the reform, including its appropriate timing, sequencing, and modalities. The PSIA consists of ex ante and ex post qualitative and quantitative analyses of potential positive and negative consequences of the sector reform for the welfare of different categories of stakeholders.Its ultimate objective is to assist the government of Chad in choosing alternative scenarios of privatizing the parastatal cotton company (Cotontchad) by taking into consideration the social and poverty impacts of each scenario.

The first stage of the ex ante analysis study was completed in November 2002. It provided the government with a sense of the overall social and economic context of the sector and the main constraints that prevented cotton farmers from geting a fair remuneration for their efforts. The revealed constraints include (i) the reduction of farmers" income, owing to unfair evaluations of the quality of cotton; (ii) long delays in payments to cotton farmers; (ii) the low quality and high cost of agricultural inputs; and (ii) the lack of information and training about cotton production and commercialization.Nonetheless, the results of the ex ante qualitative analysis demonstrated that there exists no real alternative or better source of income for the population living off cotton production, and, therefore, there is a need to address the current weaknesses through a reform of the sector. The second stage of the qualitative analysis will focus on privatization scenario-specific issues before the adoption of the final scenario. The conclusions of the ex ante analysis will be discussed in forums scheduled for July and August with cotton producers and investors on the different scenarios to be considered by the government for the privatization of Cotontchad.

The ex ante quantitative analysis was launched in May 2003. It consists of a household survey based on, and informed by, the results of the qualitative analysis. The ex post quantitative analysis is expected to be launched in the first semester of 2004 and will focus on the impact of the scenario of privatization to be chosen. Its results will serve to realign the reform program, if necessary.

18. Uneven progress has been made toward meeting the HIPC Initiative completion point conditions (Appendix IV). The full PRSP has been completed and submitted formally to the Fund and the World Bank. The condition on maintaining macroeconomic stability is being met, although additional effort is needed to increase fiscal revenue in a sustainable way and keep monetary developments under control. Some measures in the area of governance are being implemented. However, progress has been slow in implementing other governancerelated measures, such as public procurement reform and expenditure monitoring. Conditions in the area of basic infrastructure, specifically vis- $\dot{\mathbf{a}}$ -vis the road network, have already been met, but those in the areas of rural development, health, and education remain to be achieved, with some of the indicators actually deteriorating. Delays in the commitment and payment of expenditures in these areas and, more generally, the persistently weak institutional capacity in the respective line ministries are responsible for this unfavorable performance.
III. REPORT ON THE DISCUSSIONS
A. Overview

19. Discussions for the fifth review under the PRGF arrangement focused on (i) Chad's economic performance at end-2002; (ii the proposed adjustments to the program for 2003; (ii the detailed modalities for the use of oil resources; (iv) the updated macroeconomic framework (Box 3) consistent with the PRSP, and the work remaining to be done for this document to be finalized; (v) progress toward meeting completion point conditions under the enhanced HIPC Initiative; and (vi) the cooperation between Chad and the Fund after the expiration of the current PRGF arrangement. A full presentation of the understandings reached in these discussions is provided in the attached MEFP. The present report concentrates instead on the main issues that arose and on the ways the authorities and the staff approached them to arrive at the positions presented in the MEFP.
B. Revision of the Medium-Term Macroeconomic Framework

20. The revision of the medium-term macroeconomic framework mainly reflects the earlier-than-expected completion of the pipeline project and is consistent with the updated long-run framework of the PRSP (Box 3). Oil production is now expected to start as early as July 2003, and exports will begin about three months later--the time needed to fill up the pipeline and the offshore export facilities. As a result, real GDP growth in 2003 has been revised upward from 9 percent in the original program to 11.2 percent (Table 1). In the revised framework, GDP growth would reach 42.4 percent in 2004 and 15.3 percent in 2005 on account of the acceleration of oil production. Exports in 2003 have been significantly revised upward because of the initiation of oil exports. In contrast, imports in 2003 have been revised downward following the completion of a larger-than-expected part of the oil pipeline project in 2002. Exports in 2004 and 2005 will reflect the rising pattern of oil production, while imports will continue to decrease on account of the gradual completion of the oil exploitation facilities. As a result, the current account deficit, excluding official transfers, is now projected at 36.1 percent of GDP in 2003, 10 percentage points of GDP lower than the level initially programmed, and is expected to narrow dramatically to 10.5 percent of GDP and 4 percent of GDP in 2004 and 2005, respectively (Table 5). The annual average inflation rate for 2003 has been revised from 4 percent to 4.3 percent on account of the carryover effect of the higher-than-expected inflation rate in 2002. It is projected at 4 percent for 2004 and 2005.
C. Revision of the 2003 Fiscal Program

21. Decompression of nonpriority expenditure and a catch-up in priority sector spending are driving the revision of the 2003 fiscal program. The expected decline of foreign financing in 2003 had led to an earlier compression, especially of nonpriority

expenditure. It was understood at the time the fourth review that, should additional financing become available, the fiscal program would be revised. Additional financing has now been secured, thanks to the payment for signing a new oil exploration permit and an increase in budgetary support from donors (para. 18 of the MEFP). Except for the inclusion of a roughly estimated CFAF 7.2 billion in oil revenue that will accrue on account of the earlier than originally envisaged initiation of oil exports, revenue projections have not been revised, as remedial measures have been identified to correct the slippages observed in the collection of taxes in2002 (para.27 below).0ll On the expenditure side, the revision of the 2003 fiscal program reflects a catch-up for the weak execution of expenditures in the priority sectors in 2002, as well as the repayment of external arrears that have accumulated and the domestic arrears that were not repaid as expected in 2002.l2 Moreover, budgeted expenditures for 2003 in certain nonpriority areas (and, to a small extent, in the priority sectors) will be increased. As the same time, foreign-financed investment for 2003 has been revised downward on account of an updated census of projects. As a result of these revisions, the 2003 target for the overall fiscal deficit , on a commitment basis and excluding grants, was increased by CFAF 8.3 billion.l3 Regarding the financing of the revised budget, only a part of the additional foreign financing will need to be spent in 2003; the remainder will be saved in order to minimize the expected financing gap in 2004.'4 Moreover, all of the oil revenue in 2003 is currently projected to be saved, partly as a provision for debt service for pipelinerelated debts, as per the loan agreement with the World Bank.
Box 3. Chad: The Long-Term Macroeconomic Framework, 2000-20
The oil era

The initiation of oil production is now expected in the summer of 2003. In 2004, oil production will account for 34 percentage points of the total GDP growth rate of 42 percent. Under the conservative assumption that no new oil fields willbe discovered, about 80 percent of the currently identified Doba reserves will already be extracted by 2015. Oil revenues accruing to the government are projected to average an equivalent of about $\mathrm{\US{\$200}}$ million annually during 2004-15. They include royalties on oil production, taxes on oil exploitation companies, and dividends. However, given the oil production profile and the impact of the depreciation charges for the large investments on the taxable income of oil exploitation companies, the year-to-year distribution of oil revenues will be very uneven (Table 7).
Non-oil sources of growth

Overall non-oil GDP is projected to grow by an average of 6.l percent a year during 2003-15. The primary sector output is projected to grow by 4.2 percent annually, owing to an improvement in infrastructure, services, and access to inputs, as well as to the positive impact of the ongoing cotton sector reform. Secondary sector GDP, excluding oil production, is projected to increase by 7.2 percent per year over the same period. This willresult from an increase in output of cottonrelated industries, assuming a successful implementation of the cotton sector reform; an increase in productivity, thanks to lower production costs; high growth of the construction sector, boosted by investment in infrastructure; and the development of new industries on account of an improved environment for private sector development and increased demand from richer households. Tertiary sector GDP is expected to grow by 6.5 percent annually over 2003-15 as a result of the sustained growth of the primary and secondary sectors, important productivity gains, and the “catch-up" of the currently undeveloped financial sector (Table 8).
Macroeconomic stability, long-term fiscal sustainability, and poverty reduction

The macroeconomic framework is targeting an inflation rate of 3 percent per year in 2003-20, an objective that is consistent with maintaining external competitiveness, in the context of a monetary zone with a fixed exchange rate. Debt sustainability indicators are projected to improve dramatically over the medium term on account of oil production and exports, as well as HIPC Initiative debt relief. Moreover, total net external borrowing is projected to decrease significantly, reflecting an improvement in the savings-investment balance. However, around 2015,when oil resources dwindle, debt indicators will deteriorate again before stabilizing, thanks to adequate fiscal management. Similarly, Chad will accumulate significant foreign exchange reserves in the coming years, but it will need to draw for some years on these reserves beginning around 2015, before export and import-substituting sectors fully mature.Fulfillment of the above conditions for macroeconomic stability and post-oil era fiscal sustainability require continuing the efforts to generate non-oil fiscal revenue and staying on a cautious path of expenditure consistent with the absorptive capacity of the economy. In this macroeconomic framework, poverty reduction would be achieved through robust growth of per capita real income and consumption; strong increases in pro-poor public spending; and rising private non-oil investment, the latter of which is a necessary condition for continuing high income growth rates beyond the horizon of the scenario.

img_caption: Box 3. Chad: The Long-Term Macroeconomic Framework, 20o0-20 (concluded)

text: 22. The 2003 budget revision is not expected in itself to lead to additional inflationary pressures, but the risk of excessive growth in aggregate demand in the economy as a whole cannot be dismissed. To address this risk, which would endanger inflation and competitiveness, understandings were reached with the authorities that measures would be taken as needed in the monetary area (para. 30 below) and not in the fiscal area. The reasons for this policy choice are outlined in paragraph 15 of the MEFP.

23. Wage awards need to be consistent with long-term fiscal sustainability. The authorities informed the staff that an agreement had been signed with the unions to increase base wages in 2003 by 10 percent in the health and education sectors and by 5 percent in other sectors. The agreement also envisaged a base wage increase in all sectors of 10 percent in 2004, and there were understandings for a 15 percent increase in 2005. The staff expressed concern at the authorities' undertaking such commitments without prior consultation with the Fund. While recognizing that the fiscal cost of this measure was within reasonable limits for 2003, the staff cautioned the authorities about the longer-term implications of the envisaged increases for 2004 and 2005 for the fiscal accounts. An understanding was reached to include the 2003 increase in the revised budget, but to limit the increase in the overall wage bill in 2004 to 12.6 percenta level that would be consistent with the fiscal projections in the PRSP macroeconomic framework. Such an increase in the overall wage bill would correspond to only a 7.5 percent base wage increase but would allow for the recruitment of about 3,000 staff, which would help increase administrative capacity if the new personnel were appropriately allocated.

24. The deconcentration of local administration generates costs and also raises some concerns. The authorities proposed to include in the revised budget costs stemming from deconcentration-—the division into smaller units of local administrative units--in preparation for the envisaged decentralization. The staff (i) stressed that an overall strategy for deconcentration and decentralization was still missing, thus making it difficult to assess the new structure and its costs; (ii) expressed concern about the fait accompli of the nomination of personnel for the new units, and that most of the new staff was appointed outside of the civil service, and its remuneration determined by decree outside the new rules of the civil service. An understanding was reached that the full wage bill for the recently hired staff would be included in the revised budget-as the personnel were already in placebut that only part of the initially envisaged amount for nonwage expenditures would be put in the budget in order to smooth out these expenditures over time, and in view of the need to allocate funds to priority sectors in 2003, as well as to reduce the projected financing gap in 2004.

25. Expenditure on the domestic energy sector awaits further assessment. First, the authorities proposed to include in the revised 2003 budget a CFAF 2.4 billion allocation for repairing and burying the Sedigui oil pipeline before the rainy season begins. An understanding was reached that the proposed expenditure on the pipeline would be assessed by the World Bank—which has the lead in this area-before the money allocated in the revised budget can be spent (see para. 37 of the MEFP). Second, no subsidy to the water and electricity company (STEE) is currently envisaged in the revised 2003 budget, as the government and the STEE have not yet agreed on the latter's request for a subsidy of CFAF 5 billion to compensate for the high oil prices and the lag in the rehabilitation and acquisition of equipment linked to a delay in financing. In these circumstances, the budget has been revised to include only a supplementary allocation of CFAF 2 billion to cover fully the bills of the government for water and electricity in 2002 and 20o3. However, the World Bank staff will continue to discuss with the government the issue of the subsidy, and the results of such a discussion could be reflected, if necessary, in a budget amendment later in 2003.
D. Ensuring Pro-Poor Composition of Expenditure over the Medium Term

26. The authorities' commitment to execute priority expenditures is confirmed, and the measures to buttress it identified. The staff expressed its concern about the persistent problems in the execution of priority sector spending, especially given the extreme needs of Chad in this area and the current difficulties in making progress toward meeting the enhanced HIPC Initiative completion point conditions.15 The staff indicated that this performance could raise doubts about the authorities' commitment to make poverty reduction the priority guiding policy decisions. The authorities confirmed their commitment to poverty reduction and to supporting the priority sectors. A set of measures was discussed to help maintain the focus on spending in these sectors, and to implement in the short run the somewhat ambitious full catch-up of priority spending in 2003 (para. 22 in the MEFP). These measures willallow for a rapid detection and correction of slippages, mostly by enhancing the monitoring of the various stages of budget execution, centralizing commitments within each ministry, and preparing rolling three-month commitment plans. There would also be a reallocation of human resources to ensure that capable staf members are working where they are most needed. The World Bank and other donors are providing additional technical assistance to the priority sector ministries to help them prepare their budgets and ensure the latter's timely execution. However, these improvements in public expenditure management, in general, and the achievement of the 2003 spending target, in particular, are feasible only if there is a strong political commitment on the part of the government.
E. Improving Non-Oil Revenue Performance over the Medium Term

27. The imminence of oil revenue should not weaken the authorities' resolve to increase the very limited non-oil fiscal revenues. The staff expressed concern about both the weak performance of non-Doba-related tax receipts and the slow progress in reforms in the tax area. It emphasized the importance of pushing forward with reforms and vigorous tax collection, so as to establish the basis for long-term fiscal sustainability (especially after the oil era). The authorities committed themselves to starting the implementation of the customs action plan immediately, in accordance with the understanding reached by the authorities and the recent FAD mission. They also pledged to strengthen the implementation of the measures aimed at limiting the abuse of the Doba project's exemptions (para. 21 of the MEFP). In this area, as in others, strong political commitment is the key to success.

28. The authorities have been contemplating the creation of a maritime fee that would be collected on exports to Chad. The mission, together with the overlapping FAD mission, advised against the proposal. The staff felt that the strategy for increasing fiscal revenue should continue to focus on strengthening the collection of existing taxes, rather than creating difficult-to-collect new taxes. Moreover, the specific proposal would (i) not fit within the regional Central African Economic and Monetary Community (CEMAC) framework; (ii) have a collection cost that exceeds that of any other existing tax; (ii) go against budgeting principles; and (iv) earmark revenues for investment in railways, which is not a priority identified in the PRSP. An understanding was reached that, in exchange for not creating the maritime fee in 2003, Fund technical assistance would be provided to look further into the specifics of the proposal and identify, consistent with the CEMAC trade framework, possible measures to enhance trade-related fiscal revenue.
F. Monetary Conditions, Inflation Risks, and Financial Sector Issues

29. The staff expressed concern about inflationary pressures in the country—even if these are currently hard to quantify—-and raised the issue of the connection between the large increases in money and these pressures. The National Director of the BEAC and the Minister of Finance agreed that monetary growth, on account of unsterilized inflows related to the construction of the pipeline, had accommodated large price increases, which had, in turn, eroded competitiveness (see para. 7 above). Moreover, as any reasonable estimates of growth in money demand could not account for the increases in money supply in 2001-02, excess money balances may still remain in the hands of the public, thereby creating a risk of higher-than-desired inflation in 2003. The likely existence of excess money balances at end2002 was taken into account in the new monetary projections for 2003 (Table 6 and para. 24 of the MEFP), which revise downward the projected growth rate of broad money from 13.6 percent to 10.3 percent.16

30. While Chad belongs to the Central African Monetary Union, there is some leeway for monetary policy measures taken locally to be effective over the short and medium term, by the same token that monetary shocks (such as the one associated with unsterilized, pipeline-related inflows) do affect local monetary conditions and inflation in Chad. In this context, understandings were reached with both the BEAC and the Chadian government that, should rapid money growth continue in 20o3, a number of measures would be implemented (para. 25 of the MEFP), including changes in the required reserve ratio for banks (on a temporary basis), transfers of government deposits to the central bank, and sales of government bearer bonds (the proceeds of which would be deposited at the BEAC). Such measures would require the close cooperation of the Chadian government and the BEAC. Finally, understandings were reached that, in light of the lack of comprehensiveness of the current price index, the BEAC will have to cooperate closely with INSEED, the government's statistics institute, in order to improve the quality of the index.

31. The banking system is in a broadly satisfactory state. Nevertheless, some banks have to continue increasing their provisioning for bad credits and improving their lending practices with a view to avoiding the extension of new low-quality loans (para. 26 of the MEFP). The Central African Banking Commission (COBAC) has cautioned banks to follow prudent lending practices, and it has committed itself to monitoring the banks more actively. Moreover, it has asked banks to gradually raise their capital asset ratio to 8 percent by 2005, from 5 percent in 2002. With a view to strengthening the underlying liquidity position of banks, the authorities and the mission agreed that banks will have to reduce their somewhat high dependence on refinancing from the BEAC, and on deposits from the government and a few large institutions/companies.
G. Modalities for the Use of Oil Revenue

32. The initiation of oil production is now scheduled for July 20o3 and will profoundly change Chad's economic environment (Box 3, and Tables 7 and 8). To avoid the“natural resource curse" that has plagued other developing countries, and to turn this historic development opportunity into sustainable high growth and poverty reduction, Chad will have to (i) attain macroeconomic stability, including fiscal sustainability and external competitiveness, in the context of volatile oil prices and large, but exhaustible, oil revenues; and (ii) ensure the effcient, transparent, and pro-poor use of oil revenue in the context of very low administrative and absorptive capacities and an uneven governance record.

33. The authorities have committed themselves to implementing modalities of the use of oil revenues that are consistent with these goals. A unique piece of legislation, the law of petroleum revenue management (LPRM), which earmarks the bulk of oil revenue for poverty-reducing spending, was adopted in 1999 (Box 4). This legislation, together with the Petroleum Rcvenue Management Program (PRMP) described in the pipeline loan agreement with the World Bank, form the broad framework within which specific modalities will ensure that the above-mentioned goals will be achieved. Discussions between the authorities and the joint Fund-World Bank mission focused on defining these detailed modalities. The specific
Box 4. Chad: The Law of Petroleum Revenue Management

The Law of Petroleum Revenue Management, which was elaborated in consultation with the World Bank as part of the Doba pipeline project, was adopted in 1999. It applies to Chad's revenue from the three main Doba oil fields, net of debt service, and aims atdirecting most of the oil revenue toward poverty-reducing spending and ensuring the transparent management of oil revenue.
Earmarking schemes
The law earmarks Chad's direct oil revenue (royalties and dividends) as follows:'

Ten percent is saved in the Fund for Future Generations (FFG). Seventy-two percent of royalties and 76.5 percent of dividends are used to finance spending in priority sectors, namely, health, education, rural development, and infrastructure. Spending should be additional to the budget allocation of the year before the start of the oil production. A share of 4.5 percent of royalties is allocated to the oil-producing region. ● The remainder (13.5 percent) can finance the regular budget.2
Indirect revenues (upstream tax and pipeline tax) are not earmarked.3
Control and monitoring
The law provides for a number of control and monitoring procedures aimed at ensuring transparency of oil revenue management:

All oil revenue should be budgeted, and associatcd spcnding should follow normal budgetary procedures, including ex post auditing by the Accounting Office of the Supreme Court. 一 Spending financed by direct oil revenue is under the oversight of a monitoring board, whose nine members represent the authorities (Director of Treasury, National Director of the regional central bank (BEAC), and one member of parliament), the Supreme Court (one magistrate), and civil society (unions, nongovernmental organizations, human rights associations, and religious groups). The board is to play both an ex ante and an ex post controlrole and willpublish quarterly reports. Representatives of parliament and civil society are chosen by their peers for a three-year period. Accounts in which oil revenues are held (including offhore accounts) should be audited annually, and management reports should be prepared periodically.
Stabilization of spending

While the law does not explicitly provide for a protection mechanism against oil revenue fluctuations, such a mechanism is nevertheless possible under the current legal framework.For example, Article 22 of the law indicates that an account could be established to receive possble surplus income.
