understandings reached are detailed in an addendum to the TMU (Appendix I, Attachment II); an outline of these modalities is also presented in paragraphs 27-35 of the MEFP. Adoption in the form of presidential decrees of the modalities pertaining to the stabilization and sterilization mechanisms and the additionality of oil revenue-financed priority sector spending, as well as to the role of the College de Controle et de Surveillance des Revenus Petroliers (CCSRP),'7 is a prior action for the completion of this review. Below are some of the reasons for the choices made in coming up with these modalities.
Stabilization mechanism and expenditure/saving planning process

34. The stabilization mechanism will aim at ensuring an effective absorption of spending and a smooth path for that spending in the face of oil receipts that will vary across the years and vis-a-vis projections within a given year.18 Moreover, the level of spending will have to be consistent with fiscal sustainability and macroeconomic stability over the long term-defined in terms of certain indicators, as discussed in Box 3 on the longrun macroeconomic framework of the PRSP.

35. A specific concern has been the preparation of oil revenue forecasts. Both the authorities and the staff felt that these forecasts should not be subject to discretion, as they could thus be influenced by political considerations. Moreover, they should be based on internationally accepted forecasts of the market for oil, rather than on past data, which do not constitute a reliable basis on which to project future prices. To this end, oil revenue projections will be derived from a model acceptable to the World Bank and the Fund, using oil price and exchange rate projections available from the World Economic Outlook. The oil price and exchange rate assumptions would be set annually, at the time of the formulation of the government budget.

36. Another important concern has been to make the concept of absorptive capacity operational for the priority sectors (which receive the earmarked oil revenue) and, more broadly, for all sectors. This pivotal issue will be addressed by deriving annual expenditure targets from the medium-term expenditure framework (MTEF), which will be updated each year with the assistance of the World Bank on the basis of public expenditure reviews for each sector.9These medium-term projections willhave to be consistent withthe long-term macroeconomic framework of the PRSP. Currently, it is envisaged that, as low absorptive capacity in the priority sectors will be the limiting factor in the use of oil revenue in the short to medium term, this factor alone willimply the buildup of a significant cushion of stabilization reserves in the first years of oil production.

37. While the stabilization mechanism would smooth out planned expenditures over the long term in the context of projected variable oil receipts, the mechanism would also involve a within-year stabilization effect. Specifically, in any given year, any excess of actual oil revenue over budgeted revenue will be fully saved; conversely, in the event of a shortfall in revenue compared to budgeted expenditure, accumulated savings will be used up to a point to finance budgeted expenditure.

38. The modalities aim at implementing a key goal of the LPRM—the additionality of poverty-reducing expenditure financed by oil revenue. The modalities are thus based on the principle that poverty-reducing expenditure financed by direct oil revenue should be additional to poverty-reducing spending that would have been made in the absence of the oil discovery. To make this principle operational, understandings were reached that the 2002 share of budgeted priority expenditures in total budgeted expenditures (both financcd by fiscal revenues other than earmarked oil revenue) would at least be maintained over the years. If, in any given year, there were shortfalls in the implementation of priority expenditures financed from nonearmarked revenue—i.e. if the above-mentioned rule were not observed ex post-additionality would require that any shortfall in the execution of priority expenditures financed from nonearmarked resources within a given budget year will be systematically reprogrammed in the following year's budget.
Sterilization of saved oil revenues

39. A goal of the modalities has been to specify the mechanisms that would ensure the appropriate sterilization of the large savings built up in the context of the stabilization mechanism, so as to avoid excess growth of credit and liquidity in the country and thus maintain low inflation and external competitiveness. This is necessary despite Chad's membership in the Central African Monetary Union, as there may exist within the zone somesegmentation bycountryofthe market for moneyandcredit,2as wellasofthe market for goods and services. In this context, the LPRM requirement that Chadian

commercial banks receive the oil revenues could cause significant excess liquidity in the Chadian banking system if these revenues were left unsterilized. The possibility was considered of modifying this article of the LPRM, but it was felt that this might open the door to other, less benevolent, amendments to that law. The government opted instead to require that the bank receiving these oil revenues transfer them fully to a treasury stabilization account opened at the BEAC. Oil resources earmarked to finance priority expenditure would be transferred from the stabilization account at the BEAC to a commercial bank account at the beginning of each quarter, to the extent implied by the quarterly fiscal program.21
The role of the College de Controle et de Surveillance des Revenus Petroliers (CCSRP)

40. The modalities further specify the roles and functions of the CCSRP—the independent monitoring and control body overseeing the implementation of the LPRM—on the basis of the relatively general formulation included in decree 168/PR/2001. The goal was to specify that the CCSRP would be involved at all stages of the budget process, so as to ensure that earmarked oil resources were used efficiently to finance priority expenditure (as per the LPRM). At the same time, all agreed that this involvement should not create a de facto second budget administration and/or delay the already slow budget execution in priority areas. In this context, understandings were reached that the CCSRP would (i) be consulted at various stages of budget preparation, (ii monitor expenditure commitment, procurement, and service delivery, (ii) monitor all transfers out of the stabilization account at the BEAC, and (iv) authorize, on a lapse of time basis, payments for priority sectors.
The Fund for Future Generations (FFG)

41. The modalities clarify that the FFG—to which, as per the LPRM, 10 percent of direct oil revenue willbe transferred—should be seen as a long-term savings instrument that would be enhanced by allowing full capitalization of its earnings until it begins to be used. That time would arrive when annual oil revenues would no longer be sufficient to finance the needs for priority spending and when other savings, kept in the form of short-term instruments, would start declining significantly. To render this approach operational, the authorities felt that the resources of the FFG could begin to be used when oil revenue fell below l0 percent of total fiscal and nonfiscal revenue. Furthermore, to ensure that the longterm savings would continue to contribute to poverty reduction in coming generations, the amount that could be withdrawn during a given year would not be larger than the return to the FFG on its resources in the previous year and would be used only to finance expenditures in the priority sectors, as defined in the LPRM.

42. An issue that was discussed at length by the Fund and Bank staffs and the authorities was the contradiction between, on the one hand, the commitments made under the agreement between the government and the World Bank and in the LPRM-that the FFG's resources be invested in long-term instruments with an international institution-and, on the other hand, the rules of the BEAC and the CFA franc zone, which stipulated, inter alia, that the BEAC could not hold foreign reserve instruments with a maturity exceeding two years. This contradiction was resolved in March 2003 through the amendment of the regulations of the BEAC to allow for a longer maturity of foreign reserve instruments. Moreover, the BEAC agreed to“ring-fence" the foreign exchange reserves surrendered to it on account of the FFG and, together with the Chadian authorities, assign the management of the reserves to an international investment management firm to be chosen competitively by a Chadian investment committee.
H. Use of Revenues from New Oil Fields

43. The staff discussed at some length with the authorities the idea that Chad should adopt a framework for using oil revenue stemming from the future exploitation of new oil fields similar to that applicable to the Doba oil field resources and embodied in the LPRM. A framework for the use of new oil resources would preserve the broad principles of utilizing these resources primarily for priority sector spending, aided by an earmarking mechanism and an appropriate control system. The resources would be used in a manner consistent with macroeconomic stability and long-term fiscal sustainability. Understandings were reached that the broad principles of such a framework would be adopted by September 30, 2003.
I. Governance

44. To improve governance, there is a need for political commitment to the reforms and a determination to implement the rules in place. The staff commended the authorities for making progress in transparency through the publication of the conclusions and recommendations of a number of important audits. At the same time, however, the staff expressed its concern about deficiencies in implementing the recommendations of these audits (an example is provided in Box 5), the slow progress in reforming public procurement law and practice, and the delays in bringing about budgetary accountability via the timely submission of budget settlement laws. The authorities argued that the limited capacity in various local institutions was responsible for these shortfalls, but the mission felt that weak political commitment, as well as the embedded interests of some groups, may also be contributing factors. In their MEFP (para. 36), the authorities reconfirm their commitment to implement the necessary reforms in the area of governance.
J. Other Structural Reforms

45. The government will fulfill its obligations to Cotontchad and accelerate the reform of that enterprise. To avoid increasing the operating deficit of Cotontchad in 2003, and since the cost cuting plan being implemented by the company is not expected to yield significant savings, the World Bank and Fund staffs urged the authorities to (i) transfer to Cotontchad on a timely basis the subsidy programmed for 20o3; (ii) remain current in the repayment of the debt due to Cotontchad; and (ii) reimburse the VAT due to Cotontchad on a regular basis. The authorities agreed with these recommendations, as well as on the need to prepare carefully (on the basis of the results of the PSIA), and in a timely manner, the cotton sector reform. Implementation according to the established timetable of the remaining key actions in this area (para. 38 of the MEFP) could allow the completion of the reform by early 2004. Some of the remaining actions will constitute conditions for the World Bank's SAC VI (Box 6). Measures willbe taken to accelerate civil service reform and some of these will also constitute conditions for the World Bank's SAC VI. The World Bank and Fund staffs stressed the importance of making effective the merit-based salary increase, as envisaged in the reform, in order to create incentives and promote professionalism among civil servants.
Box 5. Chad: An Example of Weak Implementation of Audit Recommendations

The mission discusscd in some detail the specific problem of a contract for the procurement of 11,000 agricultural plows. The audit of the Supreme Court on the use of HIPC Initiative resources had found the contract to be in breach of procurement rules in a number of areas. The Supreme Court recommended the annulment of the contract and its relaunching. The government proceeded instead to renegotiate the terms of the contract with the same supplier and to accept the remainder of the delivery that had already been initiated at the time of the audit. The authorities argued that their approach was practical and expedited the delivery of the much-needed plows. In the event, however, the recommendations of the audit of the Supreme Court were ignored, and the procurement rules were not followed, either at the time of the first contract or at the time of its renegotiation. The mission argued that the rules would have to be followed every time, as this would ensure consistent transparency, accountability, and the rule of law, all of which would help build much-needed credibility for Chad in the area of governance and lead to greater benefits for its people over time.
K. Progress Toward the HIPC Initiative Completion Point

46. The mission felt that urgent measures needed to be implemented to avoid significant delays in reaching the completion point. The Fund and World Bank staffs, expressed concern about the widespread shortfalls, and even the reversal of the progress previously made, in meeting various completion point conditions. However, the Chadian authorities are confident that all conditions will be met by June 2004 on the basis of the measures they have identified and are already implementing (para. 39 of the MEFP). Moreover, specific steps will be taken to narrow the significant gaps in monitoring policy outcomes, especially in the education and health sectors. The staff expressed the view that, without a strong commitment from the government to urgently address all the weaknesses identified, the HIPC completion point may not be attained by June 2004.
Box 6. Chad: Structural Conditionality
Coverage of structural conditionality in the current PRGF-supported program

As indicated in paragraph 40 and Table 3 of the MEFP, the structural conditionality for the remainder of the current program (prior actions for the fifth review and structural performance criteria and indicative targets under the remainder of the third annual program) focuses on the actions needed to prepare well for the imminent oil era, and on remedial measures to address the main problems that were identified. In this context, structural conditionality pertains to (i) the adoption and implementation of modalities for the use of oil revenue that will ensure macroeconomic stability and safeguard public resources; (ii) the adoption of the detailed rules for the pricing and taxation of oil products from the Sédigui field to ensure competition and efficiency, and thus support sustainable high growth; (ii) improvements in public resource management and pro-poor spending, as well as the safeguarding of macroeconomic stability, through the preparation of medium-term expenditure frameworks; and (iv) the strengthening of external debt-service management to prevent the recurrence of payments arrears.
Status of structural conditionality in the previous PRGF-supported programs

Measures that were programmed under the previous arrangement have been implemented, albeit some with delays (EBS/99/234, 12/22/99, App. VI). Measures that constituted conditionality under the first, second, and beginning of the third annual programs under the current arrangement have all been implemented, albeit also occasionally with delays.
Structural areas covered by World Bank lending conditionality

The World Bank's structural conditionality covers the following main areas: the reforms of the civil service, the procurement system, and the cotton sector, as well as the government's privatization program. In addition, in a complementary manner, both the Fund and the World Bank have conditionality in the areas of governance and public expenditure management.
Structural reforms covered by the HIPC Initiative completion point conditions

As reported in detail in Appendix IV, progress toward the HIPC Initiative completion point conditions has been uneven. In particular, some indicators in the social sectors have deteriorated, while others have improved only marginally.

47. All bilateral agreements with Paris Club creditors were signed ahead of the endMarch 2003 deadline, except that with Russia. In view also of the delay in reaching the completion point, the authorities requested and received from the Paris Club an extension of the consolidation period from March 31, 2003 to the expiration of the current PRGF arrangement. Negotiations to receive treatment comparable to the Paris Club's from the nonParis Club creditors are still ongoing (Table 10).22 The authorities are also requesting additional Fund interim assistance under the enhanced HIPC Initiative; the staff feels this assistance could be supported on the basis of the government's express commitment to fully implement the measures they have identified to address shortfalls in meeting various completion point conditions.
L. Follow-Up to the Current PRGF Arrangement

48. The authorities expressed their wish for a follow-up PRGF arrangement in view of the capacity constraints in macroeconomic management, the need to meet HIPC Initiative completion point conditions and benefit from an extension of Paris Club interim debt relief, and the country's long-term dependence on donor resources to finance public investment. They also felt that, as balance of payments support needs would be substantially reduced in the oil era, a low-access arrangement would best fit the requirements of Chad.
IV. STAFF APPRAISAL

49. Chad's economic performance was broadly satisfactory in 2oo2, but possible inflation risks require close monitoring. GDP growth was strong, spearheaded by investment in the oil sector, but consumer price inflation was higher than programmed, owing mainly to excess liquidity resulting from unsterilized foreign capital inflows related to the oil pipeline project. The authorities recognize that high domestic inflation is a concern for external competitiveness and are committed to implementing the identified appropriate measures to address the issue. The monitoring of inflation and the potential corrective measures would require the close cooperation of the Chadian government and the BEAC.

50. Broad fiscal targets were achieved in 20o2, but more effort is needed to address weaknesses in taxation. Fiscal deficit targets were met in 20O2. However, while the overall revenue target was also met, underlying weaknesses emerged in many tax areas on account of delays in implementing revenue-enhancing reforms and the failure of past measures to prevent an abuse of exemptions granted in the context of the Doba oil project. The revenue enhancement effort should not be relaxed because of the imminence of oil revenue, and identified measures in this area need to be implemented steadfastly to ensure long-run fiscal sustainability. For the coming months, the staff urges the authorities to focus particularly on implementing rapidly and in full the customs action plan, as well as the measures aimed at limiting the abuse of exemptions. Strong political commitment will be the key to success.

51. The composition of spending remains a major source of concern. Priority sector spending aimed at poverty reduction was significantly below target in 2002, owing only partly to factors outside the government's control. The staff urges the authorities to demonstrate their commitment to, and focus on, poverty reduction by achieving the envisaged catch-up of spending in priority sectors in 2003, as well as by implementing the set of identified measures and reallocating resources to address weaknesses and capacity problems in this area. Every effort should be made to avoid a repetition of 2002, when shortfalls in priority sector spending were accompanied by robust nonpriority expenditures.

52. The staff welcomes the elaboration of an updated macroeconomic framework for the PRSP. This framework shows the path to follow so as to ensure an efficient use of variable and exhaustible oil revenues for sustainable poverty reduction and high growth, while preserving macroeconomic stability and fiscal sustainability.

53. The staff regrets the recent incident of misreporting and the emergence of new external payments arrears, but welcomes the actions taken by the government to improve external debt management. The staff believes that the measures adopted to strengthen the coordination between the debt directorate and the treasury, as well as to have the BEAC carry out the debt-service payments automatically, will contribute to strengthening debt management and ensuring timely payment of external obligations.

54. There was some improvement in governance, but more needs to be done to ensure that rules are applied. The staff commends the authorities on the leap in transparency achieved by the recent publication of the conclusions and recommendations of important audits. However, it is concerned that little progress has been made in the procurement area. To start with, it is essential that the practice of direct awarding and fractioning of contracts stop and that the rest of the recommendations of the Supreme Court audits be fully implemented. To achieve sustained gains in efficiency and transparency in public procurement, the staff urges the authorities to finalize the new procurement code in consultation with the World Bank and to implement it steadfastly.

55. Major progress was made in the preparation for the oil era with the design of a comprehensive set of modalities for the use of oil revenues, which is being adopted in appropriate legal form. These modalities aim at sheltering spending from the volatility and exhaustibility of oil revenue; ensuring the effective absorption of spending in the priority sectors; sterilizing savings accumulated for stabilization purposes; specifying the exact role and functions of the joint civil society-government body that will monitor the use of oil revenue; and ensuring the true intergenerational character of the Fund for Future Generations. The staff strongly commends the authorities on the preparation and adoption of these modalities, and urges them to steadfastly implement these modalities as soon as the first oil revenues start accruing as this will be a test of the authorities' commitment to maximize the benefits of the historic opportunity that the long-awaited exploitation of oil constitutes. It is also very positive that the spirit of the framework for the use of the Doba oil willalso apply in the case of other potential oil discoveries.

56. It is necessary to thoughtfully and in a timely manner press on with the cotton sector reform, given its macroeconomic and poverty reduction impact. In the interim, the government should honor its commitments to Cotontchad to ensure that cotton production and, thus, the livelihood of many low-income Chadians are not jeopardized. It is also important to advance civil service reform and avoid taking steps that run counter to the aim of the reform, that is, a well motivated and professional civil service operating within a transparent and efficient framework.

57. Urgent efforts are needed to ensure sustained progress toward meeting the HIPC Initiative completion point conditions. Reflecting the low spending in priority sectors in 2002, several indicators in the social sector deteriorated, while others improved only marginally. Progress in achieving governance indicators has also been slower than hoped for. Measures identified in this area include steps to improve budget execution in health and education, remove the constraints on delivering basic infrastructure in rural areas, and complete quickly the governance reforms under way. The staffs view is that, without a strong political commitment on the part of the government to address urgently all the weaknesses identified, the HIPC Initiative completion point may not be attained by June 2004.

58. In view of the broadly satisfactory performance under the program, the renewed commitment of the government to implement corrective measures in areas where weaknesses have been identified (as evidenced, inter alia, by the fulfillment of prior actions), and the good progress made in the preparation for the oil era, the staff recommends that the Executive Board complete the fifth review, grant waivers for the missed quantitative and structural performance criteria, and approve the requested additional HIPC Initiative interim assistance, as well as the extension of the commitment period under the current arrangement.
V. PROPOSED DECISIONS
The following decisions which may be made by a majority of the votes cast are proposed for adoption by the Executive Board:
A. Fifth Review

1. Chad has consulted with the Fund, as trustee for the Poverty Reduction and Growth Facility (PRGF) Trust, in accordance with paragraph 2(eee) of the three-year arrangement for Chad under the PRGF Trust (EBS/99/234, Supplement 1, 01/11/00, as amended) in order to review program implementation, obtain waivers for the non-observance of two performance criteria and reach understandings regarding the conditions for the disbursement of the ninth loan under the arrangement.

2. The letter dated June 10, 2003 from the Prime Minister and the Minister of Economy and Finance, along with the Memorandum of Economic and Financial Policies ("MEFP"), the Addendum to the Technical Memorandum of Understanding and the Amendment to the Technical Memorandum of Understanding attached to it, shall be attached to the three-year PRGF arrangement for Chad, and the letters dated November 12, 1999, July 6, 2000, April 28, 2001, September 25, 2001, December 20, 2001, March 28, 2002 and September 17, 2002 together with their respective Memoranda of Economic and Financial Policies and their respective Technical Memoranda of Understanding, shall be read as supplemented and modified by the letter dated June 10, 2003 and its attachments.
3. Accordingly, the following amendments shall be made to the three-year PRGF arrangement for Chad:
a. Paragraph 1(a) of the arrangement shall be amended by the deletion of “from January 7, 2000 to December 6, 2003" and the insertion in its place of"from January 7, 2000 to January 6, 2004".

b. Paragraph 1(b) of the arrangement shall be amended by the addition, at the end, of the following new sentence: "Disbursements under this arrangement shall not exceed SDR 42.4 million until [November 30, 2003].".
c. Paragraph 2(a) of the arrangement shall be amended by the addition, for the purposes of the ninth disbursement, of the following:
"or (vi) the floor on base expenditures in the health sector; or (viii) the floor on base expenditures in the education sector,"
d. Paragraph 2(aaa) shall be deleted and replaced as follows:

"(aaa) If the Managing Director of the Trustee finds that, with respect to the eighth disbursement, the data as of December 31, 2002, indicate that any of the ceilings and floors referred to in paragraphs 2 (a)(i) to (v) of the arrangement, as amended, specified in Table 2b attached to the Supplementary Letter and in the TMU, were not observed, or that, with respect to the ninth disbursement, the data as of June 30, 2003, indicate that any of the ceilings and floors referred to in paragraphs 2 (a)(i) to (vii) of the arrangement, as amended, specified in Table 1 to the letter dated June 10, 2003 and in the Technical Memorandum of Understanding, were not observed; or"
e. a new paragraph 2 (bbbb) shall be inserted as follows:

“(bbbb) If the Managing Director of the Trustee finds, with respect to the ninth loan referred to in paragraph 1 (ccc)(iv) of the arrangement, that Chad has not carried out its intentions by July 31, 2003, with respect to the adoption by the Council of Ministers of draft presidential decrees regarding the modalities for the operational rules of the Fund for Future Generations, as specified in paragraph 27 and Table 3 of the MEFP and in paragraphs 16-22 of the TMU; or";
4. The Fund decides that: (a) the fifth review, as contemplated in paragraph 2(eee) of the three-year PRGF ingement for Chad, is completed; and

(b) Chad may request disbursement of the eighth loan referred to in paragraph 1(ccc)(ii) of the three-year PRGF arrangement for Chad, notwithstanding the non-observance of (i) the continuous quantitative performance criterion on the non-accumulation of new

external payments arrears specified in paragraph 2(c) of the arrangement and (ii) the structural performance criterion with regard to the adoption of an action plan by the Council of Ministers on the basis of the recommendations of the international audit of the customs administration specified in paragraph 2(bb)(i) of the arrangement, on the condition that the information provided to the Fund by Chad on the performance under these criteria and on the implementation of the measures specified as prior actions in paragraph 40 of the MEFP, and in the Addendum to the Technical Memorandum of Understanding, is accurate.
B. Additional Interim Assistance under the Enhanced HIPC Initiative
The Fund as Trustee (the “Trustee") of the Trust for Special PRGF Operations for the Heavily Indebted Poor Countries and Interim PRGF Subsidy Operations (the “Trust") decides:
(a) that satisfactory assurances regarding the exceptional assistance to be provided under the enhanced HIPC Initiative by Chad's other creditors continue to be in place, and

(b) that the Trustee shall disburse to Chad as additional interim assistance the equivalent of SDR 2.85 million, which shallbe made available by the Trustee to Chad in the form of a grant that shall be paid no later than three business days after the adoption of this decision to the account for the benefit of Chad established and administered by the Trustee in accordance with Section II, paragraph 5 of the Trust Instrument; the proceeds of the grant

shall be used by the Trustee to meet Chad's debt service payments on its existing debt to the Fund as they fall due, in accordance with the following schedule: 49.9 percent of each principal repayment obligation faling due between July 1, 2003 and December 31, 2003; and 19.0 percent of each principal repayment obligation faling due between January 1, 2004 and June 30, 2004, and

(c) for the purposes of Section III, paragraph 3(d) of the Trust Instrument, the conditions for the disbursement under (b) above are that, on the basis of the information provided by Chad, Chad has met (i) all of the performance criteria applicable to the seventh, and any subsequent, loan under the three-year Poverty Reduction and Growth Facility ("PRGF") arrangement for Chad (EBS/99/234, Supplement 1, 01/l1/00, as amended), and (ii) all of the conditions specified in the decisions approving the PRGF arrangement for Chad and for completing the fourth, and any subsequent, review under that PRGF arrangement.
