text: N'Djamena, June 10, 2003
Mr. Horst Kohler Managing Director International Monetary Fund Washington DC, USA
Dear Mr. Kohler:

1. On behalf of the government of the Republic of Chad, we are pleased to transmit to you the attached memorandum of economic and financial policies (MEFP). The MEFP describes economic developments during the second half of 2o02 in the context of the third annual program supported by an arrangement under the Poverty Reduction and Growth Facility (PRGF), and outlines the government's objectives and economic policies for the remainder of 2003 and beyond. In particular, it describes in detail how Chad is preparing for the now imminent oil era.

2. As explained in the MEFP, Chad met all indicative targets at end-September 2002 and all performance criteria at end-December 2002, with the exception of the continuous performance criterion on the nonaccumulation of new external payments arrears and the targets relating to spending on health and education. As explained in our letter dated March 13, 2003 on the noncomplying disbursement resulting from incorrect information provided to the Fund by Chad at the time of the completion of the fourth review under the PRGFsupported arrangement, the accumulation of new external payments arrears was due to a persistent lack of coordination between the Debt Directorate and the Treasury.' Moreover, cash-flow difficulties owing to a significant shortfall in foreign financing prevented the regularization of these external payments arrears until early 2oo3. Unfortunately, as explained in our letter of May 23, 2003, difficulties in administering the external debt service persisted, and some payments that fell due between March $1^{\mathrm{st}}$ and April 15, 2003 were not made on the due date. Since then, strong remedial actions (including having the central bank carry out debt-service payments out of a government account opened with it for that reason) have persisted, and some new external payments arrears continued to arise through April 2003. Since then, strong remedial actions (including having the central bank carry out debtservice payments out of a government account opened with it for that reason) have been consistently implcmentcd with a view to avoiding the reemergence of arrears. Chad has

remained current on allits external obligations since April 25, 2003. At the same time, the government is committing itself to carrying out the actions identified to decisively address the causes of shortfalls in spending on health and education and, more broadly, in all poverty-reducing expenditures.

3. Two structural performance criteria were observed, but one criterion and two benchmarks were not. The two criteria that were observed involved the publication of the results of important audits caried out in 2002. A first draft customs action plan was prepared on time, but its finalization took longer than expected, and, as it was adopted with a threemonth delay, the corresponding performance criterion was not observed. The benchmark on the inclusion in the 2003 budget of the medium-term expenditure program for the health and education sectors was missed because of capacity constraints. Only draft medium-term expenditure programs were prepared, but these are being improved with technical assistance from the World Bank. Finally, building a consensus on the modalities for the use of oil resources took longer than expected, due to the complexity and importance of the issues involved. In these circumstances, the corresponding benchmark at end-February 2003 could not be observed.

4. The main focus of the remainder of the third annual program is the continuing preparation for the oil era. The government of Chad is adopting a comprehensive set of precise modalities aimed at ensuring an efficient and sustainable use of future oil resources, consistent with maintaining macroeconomic stability and increasing antipoverty spending. These modalities, detailed in the MEFP and addendum to the technical memorandum of understanding (TMU), pertain to (i) the definition of a stabilization mechanism that will ensure an effective absorption of spending and shelter expenditures from oil revenue volatility; (ii) the specification of an approach that willappropriately sterilize savings built up in the context of the stabilization mechanism; (ii) the definition of the operational rules for the Fund for Future Generations (FFG); (iv) the specification of the role and functions of the College de Controle et de Surveillance des Revenus Pétroliers (CCSRP), the monitoring and control body overseeing the implementation of the Law on Petroleum Revenue Management (LPRM); and (v) the definition of mechanisms for the distribution to, and use by, the oil-producing region of 5 percent of future royalties (after payment to the FFG). These very important modalities are being appropriately integrated into the Chadian legal framework.

5. We are pleased to report the completion of the full poverty reduction strategy paper (PRSP), adopted by the government on June 4, 2003 and transmitted to the staffs of the International Monetary Fund and the World Bank on June 6, 2003. Following the forthcoming discussion of the document by the Executive Boards of these two institutions, the government will organize a donor roundtable meeting in October 2003, with a view to mobilizing the financing needed to implement the strategy.

6. On the basis of Chad's recent economic performance and the policies for the remainder of the arrangement, including remedial actions in areas where weaknesses were identified, as described in the MEFP, the government requests waivers for the nonobservance of the two breached performance criteria and the release of the eighth disbursement under the PRGF arrangement in an amount equivalent to SDR 5.2 million. The sixth and last review of the arrangement will be completed by end-December 2003. It will focus on the execution of the budget in the first half of 2003 and the implementation of structural reforms, in particular with regard to the preparations for the petroleum era. As the three-year arrangement under the PRGF is currently set to expire on December 6, 2003, the government of Chad hereby requests the Fund, as Trustee under the PRGF Trust Instrument, to extend the period of the arrangement by one month, until January 6, 2004, so as to allow time for the last disbursement under the arrangement to be made, following the completion of the sixth review. Finally, the government of Chad requests extension of the Fund's interim assistance under the enhanced Initiative for Heavily Indebted Poor Countries (HIPC Initiative) by another year, as the previous extension expired on May l6, 2003.

7. The government of Chad will provide the Fund with all information that the Fund requests in connection with the implementation of the MEFP. The government reiterates its commitment to consult with the Fund regarding the implementation of any major policy initiatives not considered during the program discussions. During the arrangement period, the government of Chad stands ready to take any additional measures that may become appropriate to ensure the achievement of the objectives of the program. We also look forward to continuing the discussions that we have initiated with Fund staff regarding the form that the collaboration between Chad and the Fund could take after the current arrangement expires.
Very truly yours,
Idriss Ahmed Idriss Dr. Haroun Kabadi Minister of Economy and Finance Prime Minister
Attachments: - Memorandum of Economic and Financial Policies - Amendment to the Technical Memorandum of Understanding - Addendum to the Technical Memorandum of Understanding
REPUBLIC OF CHAD
MEMORANDUM OF ECONOMIC AND FINANCIAL POLICIES
N'Djaména, June 10, 2003
I. RECENT DEVELOPMENTS UNDER THE PRGF-SUPPORTED PROGRAM

1. In 2002, the government of Chad implemented a comprehensive program of economic and structural reforms. This program received support from the International Monetary Fund (IMF) under a Poverty Reduction and Growth Facility (PRGF) arrangement, approved on January 7, 2000 in an amount equivalent to SDR 36.4 millon. This amount was augmented twice by SDR 5.6 million each time, increasing to SDR 47.6 million. The fourth review of the arrangement was completed on October 18, 2002, when the Executive Board of the IMF approved the seventh disbursement under the PRGF arrangement.

2. Overall economic performance remained good in 2oo2. Preliminary data indicate that real GDP growth was strong, spearheaded by investment in the oil sector. It is estimated to have reached 9.7 percent in 2002, which was, however, 1.2 percentage points below program projections due to a weather-related decline in agricultural output. The average annual rate of inflation, currently based on price data collected in the capital city of N'Djamena, is estimated to have reached 5.2 percent, compared with a programmed 4 percent. Inflation in the country as a whole is likely to have been even higher due to stronger price increases in the regions where the oil pipeline project is being implemented. Inflationary pressures in these regions stemmed from the substantial increase in the demand for nontradable goods and services, while the supply response was modest for these products. This strong demand was supported by the significantly faster than programmed broad money growth, which reached 24.2 percent in the 12 months to December 2002, compared with 15 percent projected in the program. A larger-than-programmed increase in the net foreign assets was responsible for this development and reflected the fact that a large part of the foreign exchange inflows associated with the pipeline project were not spent on imports, as expected under the program. The rise in inflation and the nominal appreciation of the euro against the dollar led to a strong real effective appreciation of the CFA franc in 2002, roughly estimated to have been in the range of 10-12 percent. This loss of competitiveness, which has taken place even before the initiation of oil receipts, is a source of significant concern for the government.

3. The fiscal program remained broadly on track in 2002, but there were problems with the composition of spending and with payments arrears accumulation. All indicative targets and performance criteria for end-September and end-December 2002 were observed, except for the continuous performance criterion on the nonaccumulation of external payments arrears and the targets relating to spending in health and education (Table 1). The primary base deficit' was below the program target, reflecting higher-thanprogrammed revenue and an overall low rate of implementation of expenditure. The good revenue performance was on account of buoyant returns of income taxes, especially from companies connected with the accelerating Doba project, which more than offset weak collections of indirect taxes, customs duties, and petroleum taxes. The weaknesses are partly explained by delays in implementing envisaged revenue-increasing measures (para. 7), but, more important, they are likely to reflect an abuse of the exemptions from taxes granted in the context of the Doba project.

4. The low rate of implementation of spending in priority sectors caused the composition of spending to be less favorable than hoped for. This was because, despite some efforts made in this area, the early preparation of commitment plans in the priority sectors remained hampered by weak administrative capacity. This reflected the persistent generalized shortage of qualified human resources, as wellas the inappropriate distribution of human and financial resources. Moreover, the removal of staff that were found responsible for irregularities in some ministries and the reorganization that followed, while very desirable, temporarily slowed the commitment process. As a result of late commitments, some payments could not be made before the end of 20o2, which explains the lower level of 2002 budget execution on a cash basis in these ministries.

5. Implementation of expenditure in the nonpriority areas proceeded as planned, partly due to its nature and the preexisting higher capacity in these areas. Moreover, nonwage current military spending was higher than initially budgeted, following the purchase of equipment worth 0.3 percent of GDP to replace that destroyed in October 2002 during a rebel attack on Faya. Expenditure under the peace agreement signed on January 7, 2002 with the rebel movement operating in the north of the country amounted to CFAF 2.1 billion (82 percent of the programmed amount), owing to the slower implementation of the agreement with the Mouvement pour la Democratie et la Justice au Tchad (MDJT). This amount includes the payments of benefits to all individuals who surrendered. Under its general policy of national reconciliation, the government did not screen them too rigorously, so as to encourage the surrender of as many armed groups as possible. Under the same policy, an additional CFAF 0.4 billion was spent for the demobilization of rebels operating in the east of the country.

6. Net domestic arrears repayment was significantly less than programmed, and a large amount of external arrears accumulated at the time of a 38 percent shortfall in foreign financing vis- $\dot{\mathbf{a}}$ -vis the program. These problems were aggravated because the contingency plan to deal with shortfalls in budgetary resources was not implemented, due to a lack of coordination between the Ministry of Finance and the line ministries, and that the treasury cash-flow plan ceased to be updated regularly.

7. Some progress was made in structural reforms in the area of fiscal revenue collection over the medium term, but often at a slower pace than expected. Beyond the above-mentioned adoption of an action plan for reforming the customs administration, progress was made toward producing a draft action plan for improving the effectiveness of livestock taxation. A draft tax procedure handbook was prepared but was not finalized

because of outstanding legal issues. A short-term action plan for strengthening collection of income and indirect taxes is being implemented. However, measures to strengthen ex post verification of exemptions have failed to achieve expected results. Moreover, the mechanism for value added tax (VAT) credit reimbursement (virtually all to Cotontchad, the state cotton company) has not been made operational, and VAT credit due for reimbursement accumulated again in 2002 because of the prioritization of payments in the context of a difficult cash situation.

8. Some steps were taken toward improving public expenditure management, but others were delayed. On the positive side, the table monitoring the execution of the budget is now produced monthly, but still with a lag of about two months, mainly due to delays in the computerization of the expenditure circuit at the Treasury. Some initial work was done to better monitor public investment execution, as a special committee was set up within the Ministry of Economy and Finance to centralize all related information. This committee has carried out a census of all foreign-financed projects and identified significant discrepancies with the data used until now, showing that shortcomings in the monitoring of these projects are worse than assessed before. Efforts would need to be made by donors to provide on a regular basis to the government the necessary information on project execution and financing, and by the government to centralize and process this information. Preliminary drafts of medium-term expenditure frameworks (MTEFs) were prepared for health and education, albeit with a delay, and submitted to Parliament.However, these MTEFs were of insufficient quality to usefully inform the 2003 budget. In addition, no progress was made in increasing the number of civil servants and government suppliers paid through electronic bank transfers, partly because of delays in finalizing the computerization of the payroll database.

9. Significant progress was made in the area of transparency via the publication of the conclusions and recommendations of several audits, namely, the audits of (i) the five largest procurement contracts awarded in 200l; (ii expenditure financed by HIPC funds between May 2001 and May 2002; (ii) the use of the oil bonus as of end-2000; and (iv) customs administration. In addition, the terms of reference for the audit of five large contracts awarded in 2002 (randomly selected) were prepared, and the Accounting Office of the Supreme Court started in March 2003 to audit expenditure financed by HIPC funds between June and December 20o2. However, the audit by the Finance Commission of Parliament of expenditure financed by HIPC funds and carried out by the Supreme Court between May 200l and May 2002 started only in February 2003. Furthermore, a draft 2001 budget settlement law prepared by the Ministry of Economy and Finance was transmitted to Parliament before the adoption of the 2003 budget. However, it was lacking the formal assessment by the Accounting Ofice of the Supreme Court, and therefore it could not be discussed by Parliament. It is now expected that the 200l settlement law will be adopted at the time of the discussion of the 20o3 revised finance law.

10.In the oil sector, as indicated by President Deby in his letters to the managements of the IMF and the World Bank in January 20o3, the government has awarded to the oil consortium that is exploiting the Doba oil fields a new exclusive oil exploration permit

encompassing area equivalent to about 80 percent of the area covered by the existing permit, for ten years. In this context, the government and the consortium have initiated discussions on the update of the December 19, 1998 convention on oil exploration, exploitation, and transportation. In these negotiations, the government is assisted by experts recruited with financing from the International Development Association (IDA).
11. Progress was limited in the other areas of public resource management.

Following irregularities uncovered in the award of contracts financed with savings from HIPC resources, a process was initiated to prosecute officials found to have been involved in such contracts.However, the administrative and financial directors, who wil have the overall responsibility of inventory management, were appointed in all ministries. In addition, the fractioning of public contracts in order to avoid open bidding procedures has persisted, despite preventive measures. Moreover, although the current procurement code was revised as envisaged, these revisions were not enforced, and unjustified use of restricted bidding continued to be frequent. The consultation process for the preparation of the new procurement code and its associated implementation decrees was slower than expected, and these decrees could not be adopted in early 2oo3 as scheduled.

12. The reform of the cotton sector is progressing more slowly than originally envisaged. The privatization of the former soap and oil division of Cotontchad has not yet been completed, due to delays in the bidding process and the valuation of the company. The draft study on various scenarios for the full divestiture of Cotontchad was completed in early December 2002. With respect to civil service reform, progress was very limited, except for the audit of nine pilot ministries, which was launched in February 2003. The computerization of the payroll, which was expected to be completed in March 20o3, has been delayed.

13. Uneven progress has been made toward meeting the triggers for attaining the HIPC completion point. As indicated above, the full PRSP was adopted on June 4, 2003, and its implementation has started. The condition on maintaining macroeconomic stability is being met, although additional effort is needed to increase fiscal revenue in a sustainable way and keep monetary developments under control. In the area of governance, (i) slow progress is being made on the computerization of the expenditure circuit and expenditure tracking in priority sectors; (ii the fifth quarterly bulletin on public procurement was published in April 2003; and (ii) a draft of the new procurement code was prepared but needs to be finalized before being adopted by the High Interministerial Committee. The presentation to Parliament of that draft code is scheduled for June 2003 and its application decrees are currently being prepared. At the same time, as already mentioned, the 20o1 budget settlement law was not adopted by Parliament before the adoption of the 2003 budget law. In the area of basic infrastructure, the all-year usable road network reached 54.6 percent in 2002, higher than the objective of at least 50 percent. However, progress in rural development is still modest. Access to potable water reached 29 percent in May 2002, compared with an objective of 32 percent, and the number of water points for livestock holders increased only by 1.8 percent, compared with an objective of 10 percent. The share of agricultural families equipped with plows declined to 18 percent in 2002, compared with a target of 26 percent, reflecting a delay in the programmed acquisition of 1l,000 plows. Finally, progress in the

social sectors has been slow, owing to the persistently weak institutional capacity in line ministries. In this context, despite some progress made in 2o02, most of the indicators in the health and education sectors are still far from the completion point targets.
II. ECONOMIC AND FINANCIAL POLICIES FOR THE REMAINDER OF 2003
A. Macroeconomic Framework

14.Projected real GDP growth in 2003 is expected to reach 11.2 percent, up from 9 percent envisaged under the program, taking into account the earlier-than-expected start of oil production. This is now expected to start around July 2003, and exports will begin about three months later--the time needed to fill up the pipeline and the offshore export facilities. In this context, exports in 2003 have been significantly revised upward on account of the initiation of oil exports. In contrast, imports in 2003 have been revised downward following the completion of a larger-than-expected part of the oil pipeline project in 2002. As a result, the current account deficit, excluding official transfers, is now projected at 36.1 percent of GDP in 2003, 10 percentage points of GDP lower than the level initially programmed. The inflation target for 2003 has also been revised slightly upward, from 4 percent to 4.3 percent, on account of the carryover effect of the higher-than-expected inflation rate in 2002.

15. To address the risk of continuing inflationary pressures and losses of external competitiveness, measures will be taken that could help contain the rate of growth of money to a level broadly consistent with achieving the inflation target for 2o03. This policy choice for addressing excess demand pressures is informed by the following considerations: (i) there is no room for cutting nonpriority fiscal expenditures, given the needs of the government and the fact that some decompression of spending is warranted; (ii) it would be contrary to the thrust of the antipoverty strategy of the government to cut spending in priority areas; and (iii) the higher-than-programmed inflation rate in 2002 was validated by higher-thanprogrammed money growth and not a loosening of fiscal policy; meanwhile, there is evidence of persisting excess liquidity in the hands of the public.

16. Projections for the medium term have also been revised to reflect the change in the schedule of oil production. GDP growth is now expected to reach 42.4 percent in 2004 and 15.3 percent in 2005. Exports in 2004 and 2005 will reflect the pattern of accelerating oil production, while imports will continue to decrease on account of the gradual completion of the oil exploitation facilities. In this context, the current account deficit, excluding transfers, is expected to narrow dramatically to 10.5 percent of GDP and 4 percent of GDP in 2004 and 2005, respectively. A 4 percent inflation rate will be targeted for 2004 and 2005.
B. Fiscal Policy

17. The fiscal program for 20o3 has been revised to account for additional expected financing, which will allow for some increase in spending, as envisaged under the PRGF-supported program. The revision incorporates (i) an increase in priority expenditures, which goes beyond the full “catch-up" of expenditure that was not undertaken in 2002; (ii) the regularization of arrears incurred in 2002; and (ii) a moderate increase in spending in nonpriority sectors, which were excessively compressed in the original 2003 budget because of external financing constraints. Additional spending includes a wage increase of 5 percent in nonpriority sectors and 10 percent in the health and education sectors; the cost of the deconcentration of local administrative units of the government; the catch-up for the underbudgeting of the electricity and water bill of the government in 2002 and in the original 2003 budget; and a provision for additional costs that may be incurred for the completion of the Sedigui pipeline (see para. 37). There are no revisions on the revenue side of the fiscal program, except for the inclusion of a very preliminary estimate of oil revenue that would accrue on account of the earlier initiation of oil production. As a result, the primary base deficit (excluding grants) will be increased from 1.5 percent of GDP in the original program to 2.8 percent of GDP.

18. In nominal terms, the overall deficit, on a cash basis and excluding grants, is projected to increase by about CFAF 21 billion in 2003. This larger deficit will be financed by the savings stemming from the weaker implementation of the 2002 fully financed fiscal program, and by part of the additional financing of CFAF 27.7 billion (1.7 percent of GDP) identified for 2003. The latter includes (i) the payment of US\$30 million (CFAF 18.1 billion) by the oil consortium for the signing of the new oil exploration permit; (ii) a likely increase in European Union budgetary support for 2003 of EUR 10 (CFAF 6.6 billion); and (iii) a US\$5 million increase in expected World Bank budget financing (CFAF 3 billion). The government intends to save about CFAF 9 billion from this additional financing to minimize the projected financing gap in 2004. Moreover, all oil revenues are projected to be saved at this stage. A modified budget law is being adopted reflecting these amendments to the fiscal program; ² the quantitative performance criteria under the PRGF-supported program have also been revised accordingly.

19. Over the short and medium term, oil revenue is expected to exceed by a significant margin the absorptive capacity of the priority sectors that is earmarked to finance. Spending on these sectors would increase by almost 27 percent annually in the period 2004-06. Consistent with the modalities described in paragraph 30, oil revenue earmarked for priority sectors will only finance spending beyond the level that would be financed by nonearmarked revenue. That level would have to constitute a share of total spending financed by nonearmarked revenue equal to that in the 2002 budget. In accordance with the principles
described below, excess oil revenue willbe saved. Over 2003-06, total reserve accumulation is projected to amount to CFAF 70 billion.

20. In 2004, spending in nonpriority sectors will be limited by the availability of resources (which comprise fiscal revenue, excluding oil revenue earmarked for priority sectors, foreign budgetary support, and bank and nonbank domestic financing). Despite a projected 15 percent nominal increase per year in non-oil fiscal revenue—-attributable to the continuing efforts in the area of tax collection—the expected decrease in foreign budgetary support willimit the growth of spending in nonpriority sectors to 3.5 percent in nominal terms in 20o4. This target is consistent with a cautious wage policy; the total wage bill is projected to grow by 12.6 percent in nominal terms in 2004.4 In spite of these efforts and the expected financing from the Fund and the European Union, a financing gap remains to be covered in 2004. The gradual easing of the financing constraints in 2005 and 2006 will permit spending in nonpriority sectors to grow by 12.6 percent in nominal terms in both years, with no financing gaps. The overall fiscal deficit, excluding grants, is expected to average 3.9 percent of GDP during 2004 05, showing a dramatic improvement vis $\dot{\mathbf{a}}$ -vis the three years before, when it averaged 11.6 percent of GDP.

21. The government will continue to implement the fiscal measures described in the MEFP for the third annual program and strengthen measures in the areas where weaknesses have been identified. In particular, the customs action plan will be implemented steadfastly, with technical assistance from the IMF and financial assistance from development partners. The implementation will start immediately in the customs office of Ngueli. The whole set of the following measures will be implemented by end-June 2003: (i) full computerization of the customs operations and an end of manual processing of documentation; (ii) simplification of proccdures; (iii) strict limitation of access to the customs wards to customs officers and licensed brokers; (iv) application of controls selectively and release of at least 50 percent of imported goods without inspection; (v) reallocation of staff outside the Ngueli office; and (vi) granting of permission to the Chamber of Commerce and licensed brokers to open customs wards. Additional measures will be taken, with a view to preventing the abuse of the Doba tax exemptions system. These include (i) the monthly monitoring of granted exemptions through a computerized system, which will allow the automatic detection of abnormal data; and (ii) the programming of on-site inspections for all products likely to be subject to fraud. These controls willbe carried out by the exemption control brigade, which will from now on receive all information relating to imports under the Doba project. In addition, the fiscal procedures manual will be published by end-June 2003, and the final action plan for the taxation of the livestock sector will be adopted by end-August 2003. This plan will apply to the taxation of exports of livestock products the same rules as for other export taxation, including its clearance through customs administration and the collection of the tax by the Treasury, as per the Central African Economic and Monetary Community (CEMAC) customs code. No new tax, royalty, or quasi-fiscal fee will be adopted under the program without this memorandum being amended. The government's strategy in the area of fiscal revenue is to strengthen the collection of existing taxes rather than add new taxes that might not be collected effciently, or might be collected by entities with an autonomous budget, thus undermining the principle of a unitary budget. Finally, the VAT reimbursement mechanism will be made operational before end-June 2003.

22. On the expenditure side, the maximum delay in producing the table monitoring budget execution in the four stages of the expenditure process will be reduced to three weeks, and its production will be made twice a month as initially envisaged, starting with the production of the table at end-May 2003. This willbe feasible thanks to the completion of the computerization of the expenditure process. In addition, the table will be augmented so as to distinguish domestically financed from foreign-financed investment. The special committee set up to centralize all information on public investment execution willproduce a monthly report on the status of investment program execution, starting in June 2003. Furthermore, with a view to preventing the recurrence of shortfalls in the execution of spending in priority sectors, the government will enhance the monitoring and planning of such spending. Annual commitment plans have been prepared for 2003 for each priority ministry. Their execution will be monitored closely, so as to avoid any slippage. To this end, starting in June 2003, a committee comprising the General Secretaries and the Administrative and Financial Directors of the Ministry of Economy and Finance and of the line ministries in the priority sectors willexamine monthly a report on the budget execution in the priority sectors and a three-month commitment and payment plan. The latter will be updated prior to each meeting on the basis of the table monitoring budget execution in the four stages of the expenditure process (the production of which will now be more timely), the report on investment execution, and the twelve-month treasury cash-flow plan whose production and updating twice a month will resume. In addition, medium term expenditure frameworks and program budgets for all priority sectors are being prepared with technical assistance from the World Bank, France, and the European Union; they willbe finalized in time to inform the 2004 budget.

23. The government is committed to preventing the recurrence of external debt payments arrears. To this end, it decided to implement necessary institutional changes aimed at enhancing the monitoring of debt-service payments. In order to address the problem of coordination between the Treasurer and the Debt Directorate, the latter has been put under the umbrella of the General Directorate of the Treasury within the Ministry of Economy and Finance. Moreover, the government adopted a new debt-payment mechanism under which the Bank of Central African States (BEAC) makes all external debt-service payments automatically from a Treasury account that has been opened with it. This account is provisioned early enough by the Treasury so that full payments are made on time, according to a debt-payment schedule prepared jointly by the Debt Director and the Treasurer. Finally, the Debt Director and the Treasurer prepare fortnightly monitoring tables taking stock of the payment of debt service during the previous month and indicating payments falling due during the following two months.
C. Monetary and Financial Sector Issues

24. The strong money growth in 2002 and 2003 may put at risk the inflation and competitiveness objectives of Chad's economic program. The large increases in money has to some extent reflected increases in real money demand, stemming from the high growth of real economic activity, as well as expectations of higher permanent income and an acceleration of the monetization of Chad's highly unmonetized economy. Moreover, the strong increases in money supply in 200l-02 have already been reflected in excess demand and higher-than-targeted price increases, which will be difficult to reverse. Despite the estimated increases in real money demand and the higher-than-programmed inflation rate at end-2002, there may still remain excess money balances in the hands of the public, creating the risk of higher-than-desired inflation in 203. This risk has been taken into account in producing revised monetary projections for 2003, envisaging broad money growth at 10.3 percent, down from an originally programmed 13.6 percent.

25. The BEAC will monitor monetary developments against a monthly monetary program and will work closely with the Chadian government to achieve the objectives of the monetary program for 20o3. Among the instruments that will be used in this effort will be changes in the required reserve ratio for commercial banks, transfers by the government of some of its deposits from commercial banks to the BEAC, and possibly the sale of small-denomination bearer bonds by the government for sterilization purposes. The latter instrument would aim at reducing the amount of cash balances currently in the hands of the public, as well as offering competition to banks, which currently provide negative real interest rates on the deposits of their clients. These measures could also have a salutary effect on the savings behavior of the private sector by helping improve the rate of return on its savings. The BEAC will monitor various indicators, with a view to avoiding an excessive monetary tightening and a drying up of bank credit to bank-loan-dependent sectors. Moreover, given that the current price index is not comprehensive, the BEAC will continue to work with INSEED, the statistics institute, to improve the quality of the index.

26. The state of the banking system in Chad is overall satisfactory. Nevertheless, there are some weak areas (for example, a relatively elevated share of—mostly old--bad credits) that the banks need to continue to address. In this respect, some banks have to persevere with increasing the provisioning for bad credits and improving their lending practices, with a view to avoiding extending new low-quality loans. This is particularly important in the context of the large liquidity increases in 200l-02 on account of inflows related to the pipeline project. Vigilance in this area will continue to be very important as oil revenues start accruing in 2003. The Central African Banking Commission (COBAC) has been closely monitoring the banking system and will continue to do so even more actively, for example, by increasing the rate at which a bank receives on-site inspections. Moreover, COBAC has asked banks to gradually raise by 2005 their capital asset ratio to the international standard of 8 percent from 5 percent in 2002. The government and the COBAC will also work together with the banks to reduce the somewhat high dependence of the latter on refinancing from the BEAC, as well as on deposits from the government and a few large institutions/companies.
D. Modalities for an Efficient Use of Future Oil Revenue

27. The government will make every effort to strengthen the preparation for the now imminent oil era. The authorities will adopt soon--in the form of implementation decrees-the precise detailed modalities aimed to ensure an efficient use of future oil revenue for poverty reduction, consistent with the goals of short-term macroeconomic stability and long-term fiscal sustainability. The principles guiding these modalities were adopted in the context of our MEFP dated September l7, 2002. The modalities relate to (i) the definition of a stabilization mechanism and the additionality of spending in the priority sectors financed by oil revenue; (ii) the sterilization of savings accumulated for stabilization purposes; (ii) the operational rules for the Fund for Future Generations (FFG); (iv) the role and functions of the College de Controle et de Surveillance des Revenus Pétroliers (CCSRP); and (v) the distribution to, and use by, the oil-producing region of 5 percent of future royalties after payments to the FFG. The adoption of the modalities with regard to (i), (ii) and (iv) constitutes a prior action for the completion of the fifth review, while the adoption of the modalities relating to (iii) will constitute a structural performance criterion on July 31, 2003 and a condition for the completion of the sixth review. The details of the modalities are provided in the attached addendum to the technical memorandum of understanding.
Stabilization mechanism and spending planning

28. The government of Chad intends to set up a mechanism that will shelter spending from the volatility and exhaustibility of oil revenue, while taking into account the absorptive capacity of the economy, as well as fiscal sustainability and the conditions for macroeconomic stability in the long term. The main elements of the mechanism are as follows. For the preparation of the budget, oil revenue projections will be derived using a model built for this purpose from independent projections for the price of oil and exchange rates obtained from an international institution. Spending will be planned in the context of a medium-term expenditure framework (MTEF) that will be consistent with the long-term macroeconomic framework of the PRSP. The MTEF and program budgets that are under preparation in the social sectors willbe updated each year on the basis of public expenditure reviews for each sector. Revenue in excess of spending will be saved for stabilization purposes. In the event of a shortfall in oil revenue earmarked to be spent on priority sectors from expenditures to be financed by such revenue, accumulated savings will be used to stabilize such expenditures up to a certain point. Any excess in actual earmarked oil revenue above projections will be saved fully. In order to enhance the transparency of the stabilization mechanism, budget laws will specify the amount of the earmarked oil revenue projected to be saved in the course of each budget year. Any difference between the projectec and the actual amount of resources saved will have to be examined in the budget settlement law.

29. As mentioned above, the government expects to build in the first years of the oil era a significant cushion of stabilization reserves, which will permit a launching of the stabilization mechanism. The government will seek internationally competitive rates of return on these savings, which will be deposited in a Treasury stabilization account at the BEAC.
Additionality of spending in the priority sectors financed by oil revenue

30. The government is committed to ensuring the true additionality of priority expenditure financed by earmarked oil revenue, that is, that these oil resources finance priority expenditures beyond those that have to be financed by nonearmarked revenue. To this end, the 2002 share of budgeted priority expenditures financed with nonearmarked resources in total budgeted expenditures financed by nonearmarked resources will be at least maintained over time. Any shortfal in the implementation of priority expenditures financed from nonearmarked resources ("baseline" spending) will either be made up within a short period of time or matched by transfers of an equivalent amount to the BEAC account in which savings for stabilization purposes are kept, in order for the expenditure to be reprogrammed. Along the same lines, it will be important that “baseline" priority expenditures be carried out efficiently and conform to the definition used in the LPRM. Additionality, as understood above, will be certified by the Finance Commission of Parliament at the time of the consideration of the draft budget law, and by the Accounting Office of the Supreme Court in its report on budget execution.
Sterilization mechanism

31. The government of Chad is firmly committed to avoiding any situation that could cause excess liquidity in the economy and jeopardize macroeconomic stability. However, the LPRM currently requires that direct oil revenue be deposited in special accounts opened in one or more commercial banks. In the absence of any form of sterilization, this would cause significant excess liquidity in Chadian banks. In order to avoid this problem, only amounts necessary for financing budget execution in the coming quarter will ultimately be kept in one special commercial bank account; the remainder willbe kept in the Treasury stabilization account with the BEAC mentioned above (para. 29).
Fund for Future Generations

32. The government of Chad reiterates its determination to ensure the true intergenerational character of the Fund for Future Generations (FFG), to which, as per the LPRM, 10 percent of direct oil revenue will be transferred, as well as the effective use of the FFG for poverty reduction. To this end, (i) all proceeds of the fund's investments will be reinvested in it; (ii) the fund will be available for withdrawals only when oil revenue accruing to Chad falls below 10 percent of total tax and nontax fiscal revenue per year; (iii) the amount of resources that can be taken out of the fund annually will not exceed the return on the fund's resources in the previous year; and (iv) resources from the fund will be used only to finance expenditures in the priority sectors as defined in the LPRM.

33. In accordance with the commitment made in the loan agreement with the World Bank and in the LPRM-—that the FFG's resources would be invested in long-term instruments with an international institution—the government will place the FFG at the BEAC, which will, in turn, assign its management to an international investment management firm, rated at least AA by international rating agencies. The investment management firm will be chosen through open bidding procedures. The definition and monitoring of the investment strategy, as well as the choice of the investment management firm, will be subject to the approval of an investment committee headed by the Prime Minister (Head of Government) and comprising ministers in charge of Economy, Finance, and Planning, the national director of the BEAC, and another representative of the CCSRP. The FFG and its management will be subject to annual and ad hoc audits, whose reports will be widely distributed and available to the public.
Role of the College de Controle et de Surveillance des Ressources Petrolieres (CCSRP)

34. The government of Chad is determined to have the CCSRP play an effective role in safeguarding the efficient use of oil revenue. To this end, the CCSRP will be involved at various stages of the preparation of the budget, so as to guarantee its consistency with the legal framework for the use of oil resources. It will also be involved in budget execution, as it will (i) monitor expenditure commitments for the priority sectors financed by earmarked revenue, including through the conduct of ad hoc inspections; and (ii) authorize all payments from the special account for expenditures financed by earmarked revenue. The CCSRP will play a role in the ex post control of the use of oil revenues through off-site inspections of contracts and bidding process documents related to the procurement of goods and services, equipment, and construction financed by earmarked revenue, and through ad hoc on-site controls of the delivery of intended services. Finally, the CCSRP will be one of the main conduits of information to the public on the amount and use of oil resources. In this process, it will use radio, newspaper, television, and Internet communications, as well as provide free access to its reports on its premises.
Management of oil revenue earmarked for the oil-producing region

35. The government is committed to ensuring the most efficient and transparent mechanisms for the distribution to, and use by, the oil-producing region of the 5 percent of royalties that it will receive after payments to the FFG. A study has been carried out with financial assistance from the World Bank, on the basis of which the exact modalities in this area will be designed. The government will pay particular attention to ensuring the consistency between the proposed modalities and (i) the strategy defined in the PRSP, which is based on broad participatory consultations on the definition of spending priorities at the local level; (ii) the regional development plan, which is being prepared with assistance from the World Bank; and (ii) the decentralization process that the government has initiated and that will help define both the“oil-producing region" and the institutional arrangements for making decisions at the local level.
E. Governance and Other Structural Reforms

36. The government is committed to pursuing the fullimplementation of the structural reforms envisaged in the context of the third annual program and will make every effort to make up for the delays that have occurred in several areas. In the area of governance, it will continue to implement the recommendations of the various audits undertaken in 2002, and, in particular, of the audit of HIPC-financed contracts carried out by the Accounting Office of the Supreme Court. The new procurement code will be adopted by the High Interministerial Committee as soon as it is finalized, which will be done in consultation with the World Bank. In addition, all administrative and financial directors in the priority sector ministries will be appointed by mid-June 2003 (this constitutes a prior action for the fifth review). Moreover, the government reiterates its commitment to follow competitive and transparent procedures in all cases of public contracts. In proceeding further with the negotiations regarding possible new conventions for the exploitation of new oil fields, the government willseek appropriate technical assistance, with a view to maximizing the benefits for Chad. Furthermore, the government is committed to setting up in the near future the appropriate framework to ensure that oil revenue stemming from the future exploitation of new oil fields will be used in the spirit of the LPRM, namely, primarily for poverty-reducing spending and in a manner consistent with the objectives of macroeconomic stability and long term fiscal sustainability. The principles of such a framework will be adopted by September 30, 2003.

37. Regarding the oil field of Sedigui, the government has decided that the project will proceed in two phases. In the first phase, the gas and oil resources of the Sedigui oil ficld will be used to supply the national electricity company, with a view to reducing the cost of electricity. In the second phase, a minirefinery will be built in N'Djamena to supply the domestic market with refined petroleum products. The refinery willbe built and operated by a private investor to be selected following transparent bidding procedures. The government will adopt by end-September 2003 the detailed rules for the pricing and taxation of Sedigui products, consistent with the principles previously adopted. In the awarding of the rights for oil production, transportation, and refining for the Sedigui oil, bidding documents will be drafted consistent with these rules. Development of the oil field in the first phase will be carried out by the government with World Bank support. In this context, an understanding was reached with World Bank and IMF staf that the expenditure on the pipeline that was included in the modified budget law (para. 17) will be carried out only if it is assessed to be necessary by the World Bank. The assessment willtake into consideration the domestic energy policy as a whole and willbe based on the provision of full documentation to World Bank staff on all the work done on the Sédigui pipeline.

38. The government will pursue the reforms that are covered by World Bank conditionality as scheduled. In the cotton sector, the next main steps are (i) finalizing the privatization of the former soap and oil division of Cotontchad; (ii) finalizing the study of the various scenarios for the full divestiture of Cotontchad, including an analysis of the benefits and risks of each scenario, as wellas the definition of needed accompanying measures; (iii) conducting, by early June 2003, the second phase of the qualitative poverty and social impact analysis (PSIA) of the cotton sector reform; (iv) ensuring the adoption by the High Interministerial Committee of the scenarios that will be presented in the forums for producers and investors to be held in July and August 2003, respectively; and (v) completing the privatization of Cotontchad by early 2004. With a view to accelerating the civil service reform, the government will implement by end-September 2003 several actions. These include (i) the completion of the update of the census of civil servants and government contractual employees; (ii) the harmonization of this census with the payroll file; (ii the completion of the audits of the nine pilot ministries; and (iv) the publication of all the required implementation decrees.
F. PRSP and HIPC Initiative

39. As mentioned above, the full PRSP was adopted on June 4, 2003 and transmitted to the staffs of the World Bank and the IMF on June 6, 2003. Its implementation has started, paving the way for reaching the HIPC completion point in June 2004. The Chadian authorities are confident that all other completion point conditions will be met by that time. In particular, the government intends to (i) improve budget execution in education and health to ensure a satisfactory catch-up in the indicators related to this sector through the rapid implementation of the measures outlined above (para. 22); (ii) resolve all the issues hampering the delivery of basic infrastructure in rural areas; and (ii) accelerate the appointment of judges to commercial courts in the cities of N'Djamena, Sahr, Moundou, Abéché, and Bongor, so that they are in place by end-December 2003.
III. PRIOR ACTIONS FOR THE FIFTH REVIEW, PROGRAM MONITORING, AND FOLLOW-UP OF THE CURRENT ARRANGEMENT
40. The following measures constitute prior actions for the completion of the fifth review:

adoption by the Council of Ministers, in the form of presidential decrees, of the modalities for oil revenue management, as specified in the attached addendum to the TMU, regarding the stabilization and sterilization mechanisms, the additionality of spending in the priority sectors financed by oil revenue, and the functioning of the CCSRP;
clearance of all external payments arrears;

full implementation of all measures identified with a view to preventing the reemergence of external payments arrears, namely, (i) production twice a month by the Debt Director and the Treasurer of tables showing debt-service payments during the previous month and indicating maturities faling due in the next two months; (ii)

full implementation of the reorganization of the Ministry of Economy and Finance regarding the administrative structure for the payment of external debt service, as evidenced by the effectiveness of the implementation decree and the appointment of staff to all concerned positions; and (iii) the setting up of a new mechanism for external debt payment, under which the government requests BEAC to automatically make all external debt-service payments from a specific Treasury account (at the BEAC) that the Treasury provisions well in advance of when any debt-service payment is due; and
nomination by the government of all administrative and financial directors in the priority sector ministries.

41.The sixth review under the PRGF arrangement is expected to be completed by endDecember 2003. It will focus on the execution of the budget at end-June 2003 and the implementation of structural reforms, in particular regarding the specific measures undertaken in the preparation for the petroleum era. Revised quantitative performance criteria and indicative targets are set for end-June 2003. Quantitative performance criteria comprise (i) a ceiling on the increase in net credit from the banking system to the central government; (ii) a floor on the primary base fiscal balance; (ii) a floor on total fiscal revenue (excluding grants); (iv) the nonaccumulation of central government external payments arrears; (v) a ceiling on new medium- and long-term nonconcessional external loans contracted or guaranteed by the government; (vi) a ceiling on net change in external debt with a maturity of less than one year; (vii) a floor on base expenditure on health; and (vii) a floor on base expenditure on education. A ceiling on the total wage bill(including military) will constitute an indicative target. In addition, the reform measures indicated in Table 3 of this attachment will serve as structural performance criteria and benchmarks.

42. The Chadian government looks forward to continuing its partnership with the IMF beyond the current arrangement. It will pursue the discussions it has initiated with Fund staff regarding the form this partnership can take. In particular, it will explore the possibility of a low-access PRGF arrangement. In addition, the government of Chad would appreciate the opportunity to continue benefiting from the Fund's technical assistance, in view of the significant challenges that lie ahead regarding macroeconomic management in the context of large oil revenues and the still-limited administrative capacity of the Chadian authorities in these areas.

text: INTERNATIONALMONETARYFUND
CHAD
Amendment to the Technical Memorandum of Understanding for the Third Annual Program Under the Arrangement Supported by the Poverty Reduction and Growth Facility
Paragraph 6 is modified as follows:

6. Reporting requirements. On a monthly basis, the following indicators will be provided through, inter alia, the provision of treasury balance sheets, with a delay not exceeding 30 days: (i) government revenue, total and main components; and (ii) primary base expenditure, by nature and by ministry. The monthly monitoring tables that track spending through the four stages of the streamlined expenditure process will be transmitted within three-weeks after the end of each month. Detailed data on the repayment of domestic arrears and information on the remaining stock of arrears carried over from previous years will also be transmitted on a monthly basis, within 30 days from the end of each month. Tables showing external debt service payments actually made during the previous month and expected to fall due during the following two months, by creditors, will be prepared the 1st and $15^{\mathrm{th}}$ of every month by the Debt Directorate of the Ministry of Economy and Finance and the Treasurer and transmitted to Fund staff within a maximum delay of one week.
The former paragraph 11, which is not applicable anymore, is replaced by the following one:

11. The ceiling on the cumulative change from January 1, 2002 in net credit to government from the banking system will be adjusted downward by the amount of bearer bonds that are held by the nonbank public.

INTERNATIONAL MONETARY FUND CHAD Addendum to the Technical Memorandum of Understanding for the Third Annual Program Under the Arrangement Supported by the Poverty Reduction and Growth Facility—Modalities for the Management of Oil Revenue

1. This addendum provides details on the understanding reached between the government of Chad and the International Monetary Fund on the exact modalities for the management of the future oil revenue stemming from the exploitation of the Doba oil fields. The government intends to adopt these modalities as part of, and as conditions for, the third annual program under the arrangement supported by the Poverty Reduction and Growth Facility (PRGF). These modalities are framed in the Law for Petroleum Revenue Management (LPRM) and the loan agreement with the World Bank for the financing of the pipeline project. They follow the principles adopted in the memorandum of economic and financial policies for the third annual program related to the fourth PRGF arrangement review.
A. Prior Action for the Completion of the Fifth Review

2. The adoption by the Council of Ministers, in the form of presidential decrees of the LPRM, of the modalities for oil revenue management regarding (i) the planning of expenditure and stabilization mechanism; (ii) the sterilization mechanism; and (ii) the role and functioning of the College de Controle et de Surveillance des Revenus Petroliers (CCSRP) constitutes a prior action for the completion of the fifth review under the PRGF arrangement. These modalities will comprise the following elements.
Stabilization mechanism
3. The stabilization mechanism will be based on the use of the difference between actual revenues and budgeted spending.
4. To shelter spending in the priority sectors² from the volatility of oil revenues, the mechanism will function as follows:

a. Any excess in actual oil revenue earmarked for spending in priority sectors above projections will constitute a surplus, which will be fully saved and placed in an account opened to this end with the Bank of Central African States (BEAC) (paras. 9 and 12). The saving of the surplus will be systematic, and there will be no revision of the spending program on account of this saving.

b. However, if actual oil revenues earmarked for spending in priority sectors fall below the level of spending that they have to finance, accumulated earmarked oil revenue savings will be used to offset the ensuing shortfall. In case of an important and long lasting shock, the macroeconomic framework and the medium-term expenditure framework will be revised, and the spending program will be adjusted accordingly. A shock is considered to be long lasting and important when the actual revenue from Doba oil has been lower by more than 20 percent than the projected revenue for more than three consecutive months.

5. Oil revenue projections will be based on the projections for the price of Doba oil derived from the IMF projections for the euro-dollar exchange rate and for the price of Brent oil, to which a markdown for the lower quality of Doba oil will be applied. The latest available projections for oil prices and exchange rates will be used at the time of the elaboration of the following year's budget. The projections of oil revenues will be made using a model run by a joint Ministry of Economy and Finance and Ministry of Petroleum team; the ultimate responsibility for determining oil revenue will fallto the Ministry of Economy and Finance. Pending the development of the authorities' own model, the model to be used will be the one originally developed by the oil consortium that is exploiting the Doba oil fields. The CCSRP will have access to the model.

6. As per the LPRM, spending on priority sectors financed by oil revenues will be determined in the context of a medium-term expenditure framework (MTEF) and program budget for each priority sector, and will be consistent with the long-term macroeconomic framework of the poverty reduction strategy paper (PRSP). In particular, the spending program will take into account the absorptive capacity of the priority sectors. For the 2004 budget, the MTEFs and program budgets willbe prepared with technical assistance from the World Bank, the European Union, and France. They will subsequently be updated annually on the basis of yearly public expenditure reviews for each sector, conducted early enough in the budget preparation process to inform the budget framework paper.

7. Spending programs for all other sectors, which are financed, inter alia, by nonearmarked oil revenues, will also have to be consistent with the objectives of long-term macroeconomic stability and fiscal sustainability. Eventually, they will also be prepared in the context of MTEFs. By definition, all revenues in excess of spending will be saved. In order to ensure the appropriate monitoring of the use of earmarked oil revenues, savings that may stem from nonearmarked fiscal revenue, including indirect oil revenue, will be placed in an account opened with the BEAC, distinct from the one mentioned in paragraphs 9 and 12.

8. In order to ensure the additionality of priority expenditure financed by oil revenue earmarked for spending in priority sectors as per the LPRM, the 2002 share of budgeted priority expenditures financed with nonearmarked resources in total budgeted expenditures financed by nonearmarked resources, namely, 42.6 percent, willbe at least maintained over time, starting with the 2004 budget. In order to guarantee the good execution of these latter expenditures, any measure that may be taken to freczc or limit spending will exclude priority sector spending. Any shortfall in the execution of priority expenditures financed from nonearmarked resources within a given budget year willbe systematically reprogrammed in the following year's budget.
9. For the transparency of the stabilization mechanism, a stabilization Treasury account will be opened in CFA francs with the BEAC (see para. 12).

10.Budget laws will specify as a specific transfer item the amount of the oil revenue earmarked for spending in priority sectors projected to be saved in the course of each budget year, that is, the difference between projected oil revenue earmarked for spending in priority sectors and the corresponding projected spending.
Sterilization mechanism

11. The LPRM requires that direct oil revenue repatriated to Chad be kept in special accounts opened with one or several commercial banks. In this context, only one bank will be chosen. In addition, the repatriation of oil revenue from the offshore escrow account will be made through the central bank, in accordance with the foreign exchange system rules in the Central African Economic and Monetary Community (CEMAC) zone.

12. The following mechanism will ensure a not too costly and efficient sterilization of the savings accumulated for stabilization purposes. In the first stage, all direct oil revenues, excluding the allocation to the FFG, will be repatriated to the chosen commercial bank through the central bank. In the second stage, the commercial bank will transfer allthe funds it received to the stabilization Treasury account to be opened with the BEAC. In the third stage, the BEAC will transfer (i) 5 percent of the royalties to a special account for the oilproducing region opened with the BEAC; (ii 15 percent of total funds received to a regular Treasury account with the BEAC until December 31, 2007; and (ii) on a quarterly basis, the amount necessary for the execution of priority sector spending out of earmarked direct oil revenue to the special account with the commercial bank. The contract between the Ministry of Economy and Finance and the commercial bank will specify explicitly the following: (i) the commercial bank's obligation to transfer to the stabilization Treasury account opened with the BEAC the entirety of the funds it received; (ii) the amount of the commission that the commercial bank will be paid; and (ii) the remuneration of the deposits in the special commercial bank account. The contract will also have to stipulate that the special commercial bank account can be fed only by earmarked oil revenues. The commercial bank will be chosen through a bidding process, in consultation with the World Bank as per the loan agreement, so that the government can obtain the best contract along the lines described above. Only the three best-rated banks by the Central African Banking Commission

(COBAC) will be allowed to bid. The contract will also have to be approved by the CCRSP. The contract between the Ministry of Economy and Finance and the central bank will have to specify the following: (i) the amounts to be transferred quarterly to the special commercial bank, consistent with the projections for the budget execution; (ii) the percentage of the funds to be automatically transferred to the oil-producing region account and to the regular Treasury account; and (ii) the remuneration of the stabilization Treasury account; and (iv) that transfers from the stabilization account with the BEAC will only be authorized to the account opened with the BEAC for the oil-producing region up to 5 percent of received royalties; to the regular Treasury account with the BEAC up to 15 percent of received royalties and dividends until December 31, 2007; and to the special account in the commercial bank. The contract will have to be approved by the CCRSP.
13. Indirect revenues repatriated to Chad from the offshore escrow account will be directly deposited in a treasury account with the BEAC, so as to ensure their automatic sterilization.
Role of the College de Controle et de Surveillance des Ressources Petrolieres

14.In order to play an effective role in safeguarding the efficient use of oil revenue, the College de Controle et de Surveillance des Ressources Pétrolieres will perform the following functions, which will be reflected in its operating manual and integrated into the Chadian legal framework in order to ensure the cooperation of government entities:

(i) The CCSRP will be involved in three stages of the budget process: It will participate in consultations with the Ministry of Economy and Finance and priority sector line ministries on the formulation of the latter's budgets for the upcoming fiscal year, at the time of the updating of the MTEF. It will provide a formal opinion to the Council of Ministers on the consistency of the budget proposal prepared by the Ministry of Finance for the coming year with the framework for the use of oil revenues. To this end, the CCSRP will have to receive the draft budget proposal at least ten days before the latter's scheduled discussion by the Council of Ministers. It will produce an assessment of the consistency of the draft budget approved by the Council of Ministers with the framework for the use of oil revenues. This assessment will be provided to the parliament before it discusses the proposal. The assessment will also be made public at that time.   
(ii) The CCSRP will have the right to conduct on-site inspections in the Ministry of Economy and Finance and line ministries in the priority areas, with a view to ensuring that committed expenditures to be financed with earmarked oil revenues are in line with the approved budget. The inspection could take the form of controlling the processing of commitments within a certain time
period or the processing of commitments in a certain area of expenditure, as deemed appropriate by the CCSRP.

(iii) The CCSRP will approve the contract between the Ministry of Economy and Finance and the commercial bank on the management of the special account with that bank and receive copies of the statements for all the operations on the stabilization Treasury account opened with the BEAC and the special account in the commercial bank. It will also approve the contract between the Ministry of Economy and Finance and the BEAC regarding the stabilization account and the management of earmarked funds received in the stabilization account from the special account.

(iv) The CCSRP will authorize payments for all expenditures financed by the special account in the commercial bank. It will control the consistency of payments with approved budget allocations. To avoid undue delays, the CCSRP willbe given five days to either approve or reject an expenditure. Failure to do so would enable the Treasurer to authorize the payment without the CCSRP's explicit approval. Should the government contest a decision taken by the CCSRP to reject the payment of an expenditure that the government considers legitimate, the case will be referred to the Supreme Court for arbitration.   
(v) The CCSRP will have the right to inspect any contracts pertaining to the procurement of goods and services in priority areas, and it will receive in a timely manner all documents pertaining to the solicitation, the opening, and the evaluation of bids, as well as contract awards.   
(vi) The CCSRP will have the right to conduct on-site inspections to satisfy itself that any expenditure financed by earmarked oil revenues results in the actual delivery of intended services.   
(vii) The CCSRP will be one of the main conduits of information on the amount and use of oil resources. The publication process will be made via radio, newspaper, television, Internet, and free access to its reports on its premises.

15.The CCSRP will employ a small team of core technical staf that will provide the depth, continuity, and institutional knowledge needed to carry out its work. The team will be large enough to be responsible for the payments authorization phase. The CCSRP will have the possibility of hiring consultants or interns to supplement its permanent staff.
B. Conditions for the Completion of the Sixth Review
Fund for Future Generations

16.The adoption by July 3l, 2003 by the Council of Ministers, in the form of an implementation decree of the LPRM, of the following modalities for the operational rules of the Fund for Future Generations will constitute a structural performance criterion. The modalities will comprise the following elements.
17. All proceeds from the fund's investments shall be reinvested in the fund, so as to ensure the growth in real terms of its resources.
18. The fund will be available for withdrawals only when oil revenue accruing to Chad falls below l0 percent of all revenue.

19. The amount of resources that can be withdrawn from the fund annually will not exceed the return on the fund's resources in the previous year. As per the LPRM, the resources from the fund will be used only for expenditures in the priority sectors defined in the LPRM.

20. The FFG will be kept with the BEAC under the following conditions: (i) amendments to Article 1l of the BEAC statutes decided upon during the March 2003 Council of Ministers effectively waive any restriction on the term structure of the FFG's assets, so that they can be invested in long-term instruments; (i) the FFG's assets will be managed separately from the overall BEAC reserves, which are managed by the market operations unit of the BEAC; (iii) the financial institution to which the management of the FFG's assets will be subcontracted will be chosen competitively among institutions short listed by the BEAC-- and carrying at least an international AA rating-in consultation with the World Bank as per the loan agreement. The contract between the selected institution and the government of Chad willspecify the characteristics of the portfolio (structure, risk and returns objectives, etc.) and will have explicit clauses on access to information—defined in consultation with the World Bank as per the pipeline loan agreement—-and on the preparation of audit reports (systematic annual audit reports and ad hoc audits). The contract will be signed for two years. The choice of the investment company, as well as the definition and monitoring of the investment strategy, will be subject to the approval of an investment committee headed by the Prime Minister (Head of Government) and comprising ministers in charge of economy, finance, and planning, as well as the National Director of the BEAC and another representative of CCSRP.

21. Should the BEAC not fulfill the above-mentioned conditions, the government could recruit directly, through a competitive international open bidding procedure, a financial institution to manage the FFG, in compliance with the principles laid out in the preceding paragraph.

22. In any case, audits of the performance and the management of the FFG will be systematically submitted to the government, the Parliament, the CCSRP, and the World Bank-as per the pipeline loan agreement-—-and will be widely disseminated to the public. The investment committee, the Parliament, and the CCSRP will have the right to request ad hoc audits of the performance and the management of the FFG.
